Pick up a distillation apparatus, the heating mantle, n- hexane, a vacuum pump, a purging chamber (you can buy these pre-built or make your own) Run your hexane through your distillation apparatus, collect the hexane that comes through between 150-160F. bust up your weed, pour your hexane over it, shake for about 30sec (you don't have to worry about picking up chlorophyll, n hexane is 100% non-polar. you only need a few sec per wash because it's one hell of a solvent) pour it through a coffee filter load it into your boiling flask and cook off 90ish% of your hexane, collect it in your receiving flask, because that shit is expensive ( $60/gal); and you can reuse it as many times as you like pour the contents of your boiling flask into your purging dish and let the remainder of your hexane evaporate, then purge (do all of this outside. hexane is scary flammable and the vapors are heavy, they can crawl on the ground and surfaces and find ignition sources) alternatively, you can do this with ice cold Everclear. you can dry it further with molecular sieves, which are reusable bring your purged extract inside and from there you can do whatever you want to do with it. winterize with a QWEtho to make shatter, decarb in mct to make tincture, run dab liquidizer to make carts, make conc budder or bake into edibles, etc

I've also found hemp flower to be the most effective way of using CBD. I highly recommend buying a dry herb vaporizer so you can vaporize your flower instead of smoking it. It's much healthier than smoking and you'll get much more mileage out of a small amount of flower. Smoking is the most wasteful method of consumption because you're burning up most of the flower and cannabinoids before you can even make use of them. It's also going to smell an awful lot and the smell from the smoke will linger on your fingers and clothes. Vaporizing your flower won't cause much of a smell for the most part. With the dry herb vape you can also save the leftover flower when you're done vaping and then eat it or use it to make edibles once you have several grams saved up. You can get a good dry herb vape for between $75 and $135 these days. You don't need anything complicated. Even something with just 3 pre-programmed temperature settings will get the job done well. Hemp flower usually contains more cannabinoids than just the CBD so you're probably noticing the effects of those in addition to the CBD in the flower. Smoking and vaporizing your flower causes the cannabinoids to immediately enter your blood stream to cross the blood brain barrier, causing immediately noticeable effects. As for why CBD gummies and tinctures didn't work it could be that you're one of those people who doesn't process oral/edible CBD and other cannabinoids very efficiently. You may simply need a higher dose. It's also possible that you didn't ingest the CBD with enough fat. Unless you eat CBD and other cannabinoids with something very fatty your body won't really be able to make much use of it. I've never personally used tinctures before, but usually you need to hold them in your mouth for 3-to-5 minutes or so to allow it to absorb sublingually under your tongue. Plenty of places sell CBD oil gel capsules. Those may work better for you if you're in need of something longer-lasting to help your anxiety. After you take your dose it may take up to 1-to-3 hours to kick in, but it'll last for many hours. Be sure to try those when you have a free day and don't drive anywhere until you know how they affect you. Depending on your dose you may feel a little drowsy or too relaxed.

Edibles which use fat like butter or oil, think baked goods, tend to take 30 min to two hours to be absorbed and take effect. The high for this reason can last hours. Edibles made using an alcohol based tincture, think most candies and drinks, are absorbed much faster by the body and can take effect within minutes. The high for this reason can also last a shorter period of time.

Coughing up anything that isn't just mucous and immune cells is generally a bad sign, your lungs are trying to clear stuff out because they're irritated. This isn't uncommon in daily smokers of any organic matter, and it is probably just a mix of lipids (plant oils) and tar. Wouldn't show up on an x-ray either. The good news is you're young, so your body's healing and detoxification processes are at their most effective. I've seen numerous reports over the years of people saying there was an increase in coughing up the 'black crap' when they quit. This is probably recovery of the cilia - tiny hair-like structures that line your airway and throat to help expel mucus thickened by immune response to pathogens or foreign matter - like tar. If you wish to continue using the herb for its psychological and medical benefits, I always advocate for alternative methods of administration. Oral/sublingual oils and dry herb vaping are the most common healthier alternatives. You'll thank yourself when you're older for kicking the combustion habit. You’re mixing up terms and concepts here- Carts- that’s the cartridge. It’s what holds the oil- that can be distillate, resin, or rosin. Distillate is the crudest form- it’s extracted THC from dried plant material, often with artificial terpenes added back in. Resin is usually a whole plant extract (whole plant fresh frozen- WPFF), and rosin is hash that’s been heated under pressure. They’re going to go up in terms of price, distillate being the cheapest. Dispo just means dispensary and 510 is the thread measurement on the bottom of the cart.

You can find it in most any of the health journal sites (Healthline good start) that talk about oil-based tinctures or just google 'bioavailability of CBD oil tinctures'. Pretty universal and they group it in the same category as vitamins at 5-10% efficacy. However, CBD is lipophilic (fat soluble) and therefore must travel through the liver/gallbladder, intestine and full GI tract. Consensus is the degradation nets out \ 10% bioavailable. So that 20mg tincture nets you about 2mg of CBD...hardly enough to make any difference. They are just very inefficient. That's why gel caps work 50% better...right to the stomach and can then break down. You can leave it under your tongue longer (3+ mins), but as a fat/lipid it just cannot break down enough with saliva alone. And who is going to leave it under the tongue that long. Most is swallowed, period.

These recipes use oil so it makes it easy. 1- decarb your weed. Set the oven at 240F or below. Grind your weed and place into an aluminum foil pocket (this has worked best for me). Bake for 20 minutes/ flip/ bake for 20 minutes again. 2- remove from the oven. Let it cool COMPLETELY. Measure the oil out for the recipe you’ll be baking. 3- place baked weed and oil on a double boiler on the stove. One pot of water on low/ medium heat (just enough for steam), with a bowl on top for the oil and the weed. Do not use directly heat of the stove. This will take 6-8 hours. The longer you double boil, the more potent. 4- strain the weed from the oil. Use the oil to bake the brownies. 5- eat and enjoy. Happy baking!

Yeah, in the case of extracts there is basically no bottom to where the prices can end up. I've mentioned it many times in this sub but the best indicator is corn oil because the plants are similar in terms of production. Using liters to keep the math simple, we can see that liter of corn oil is well less than four bucks. That's a thousand grams of very high-purity oil extracted from a plant that is similar to cannabis for four bucks. That is a very real-world example. How can corn oil be so cheap? Easy --grow thousands of acres at time outdoors on cheap land with rainwater irrigation using industrial fertilizers like ammonia and then extract it with clean hexane which is the major component of gasoline. There's nothing dangerous or unhealthy about the process. I mean I'm not saying monoculture crops are a good thing but the product is not unsafe for use. The end product is very high quality with little to no impurities and the price when done at industrial scale is four bucks a liter or, in other words, a half of one US cent per gram --retail.

Yeah honestly I’d suggest just buying a full spectrum CBD cartridge if you want to vape CBD. The cartridges are much more concentrated. This is designed basically for people who use vape E-cigs as an alternative. It’s rather diluted at 1000mg per 30ml bottle. For a comparison, most vape carts will contain 1ml and are typically around 60-80% CBD. So a 1ml CBD vape cart will contain around 600-800 mg. This bottle is 30ml and contains 1000mg, that’s like 3-4% CBD by volume. So you’re gonna need to vape a lot more of this than you would with an actual CBD cartridge. It depends on the person, but a much better option for CBD intake would be dosing a CBD oil orally, imho. Or if you wanted to vape, picking up a dry herb vaporizer and buying CBD dominant hemp flower. You could also buy CBD concentrate and use a dab pen, or dab rig if you wanted. Most people who use CBD oil orally will take anywhere from 20-500mg daily. Most will start around 10-20mg and work their way up, till they find an effective dose. I personally use all these methods. I take CBD oil doses twice a day, between 100-200mg total daily. Then I also enjoy vaping hemp flower. I buy both CBD and CBG dominant hemp flower from online vendors. Then I consume like one would marijuana. Both hemp and marijuana are both cannabis, just the legal distinction is hemp is any cannabis with less than .3% THC by weight. So I vape it in a dry herb vape, smoke joints, out of a bowl or bong, make edibles, etc. I use around .3-1g of flower a day. It’s the most effective way to use CBD in my opinion. The effects are much more pronounced than something like a CBD cartridge. I also make my own CBD cartridges. I’ll buy CBD distillate online, then add terpenes I’ve bought online as well, and fill Ccell style 510 cartridges that work with any 510 threaded battery. They work but not nearly as well as the flower or CBD oil orally. Idk how familiar you are with CBD, or it’s use, but these vape juices are more of a novelty/replacement for nicotine targeted at people who already use E-juice. The ingredients will be exactly like typical E-juice, they just replace the nicotine content with CBD isolate. So it’ll contain PG/VG, food flavorings for taste, and then CBD isolate instead of the nicotine. So unless you already vape E-juice, I’d suggest one of the other routes I listed above. You’ll get much better effects, and won’t have to take a ton of hits for a decent dose.

Totally fair to be anxious getting into CBD. There's a lot to know and a ton of information to sort through. I'll do what I can to answer your initial questions here, but if any more come up, feel free to ask :) As far as what we know so far, CBD has [very few side effects]( those mostly being things like digestive issues, drowsiness, and appetite changes. The research out there seems to support the idea that our bodies can handle [really high amounts]( of CBD - far more than what your average user is taking. Full Spectrum CBD does contain some THC. It's usually a low enough amount and won't get you high, but some people are more sensitive to it than others. There's also Broad Spectrum or Isolate, both without THC. When it comes to good places to buy CBD, definitely be on the lookout for certificates of analysis (COAs). These detail the chemical makeup of the product, as well as bacteria, heavy metal, etc. levels. Transparency is also good. Also, any companies that make substantial health claims usually aren't on the up and up - we don't necessarily have the science to make these strong claims yet. We've seen a lot of great recommendations on here, so if in doubt about a product, definitely ask. In terms of smoking/vaping/edibles/oils/etc - it's kind of up to your preference. Some ways are [better absorbed]( by your body than others, but at the end of the day, it comes down to what works for you. As another note, CBD does [interact with]( certain medications. Be on the lookout for grapefruit warnings if this applies to you. I hope this helps! Again, feel free to ask if you have any other questions :)

Vaping is the best way to take your CBD for instant effects and getting the most bioavailability. It will bypass using your liver to breakdown the CBD to enter it into the bloodstream via the alveoli in the lungs. Tinctures use MCT oil to help bypass the digestive tract to help save time and more bioavailability of the CBD by sending it more directly to the liver to be processed and then entered into the bloodstream. Edibles are the slowest time and lowest bioavailability for CBD effects. The CBD has to go through the digestive tract first, then to the liver and then to the bloodstream. The digestive tract will heavily destroy things like cannabinoids and vitamins, so the full amount you take in will not be the same amount that finally reaches your liver and then your bloodstream. Edibles are the worst way to get the most out of your cannabinoids! I personally feel they are a waste of time and money. I liken it to the bright yellow pee you get after taking a bunch of vitamins...you are just peeing out a bunch of unused minerals and money. THC is a good example of this effect. If you vape THC you feel those effects instantly and powerfully, but if you take THC via a tincture you will notice it takes about 10-15 minutes to feel some effects and it will be different and less powerful than if you vaped THC. Dose tends to be very individual as everyone's body is different. The general idea is to start low and go slow, gradually increasing as your body adjusts. 15mgs isn't a large dose for sure, but it's not a bad place to start out with. Depending on what people are looking to treat, we've seen people on here reporting needing doses as small as 20-25mgs (or lower), to upwards of 100s of mgs. It really depends on a lot of factors, so unfortunately, this is a bit of trial and error. 15mg capsules aren't a bad thing to start out with though! In terms of bioavailability (how much is being absorbed by your body), capsules and other edibles [have been shown]( to be less bioavailable than other methods, like tinctures or inhalation. The liver thing you mentioned is legit - something called [first-pass metabolism]( Edibles also might be slower acting but last longer. Regardless, it usually comes down to personal preference. There's been some [research]( that suggests eating fatty foods (avocados, fish, peanut butter) with edibles can increase bioavailability, so that might be something to try as well. I know this doesn't really provide any suggestions or concrete ideas, but I hope it helps give you some ideas of what your wife wants to start with. Good luck!

I'd eat one and a half. Wait about an hour then start adding by half every hour as needed. Because they're gummies I'd highly suggest eating something a little fatty with or before the gummies. This will help absorption, and curb munchies. Otherwise sometimes what happens is we take em, doesn't absorb great, we take more, get the munchies and eat something that helps it absorb and then bam it all activates. Rules of thumb on peak activation times: Inhalation(any kind): 15mins Oral(oils,sprays,tabs): 30mins Ingestable(food stuffs): 2hrs Topical(creams/lotions): 15-30mins (depends on the carrier oil/cream and cannabinoids) Now this isn't to say you can't start to experience effects sooner or later than those ranges but that's the average. Example a newbie hits a joint real good, feels it in 3 minutes, but if they don't hit again and wait out the full 15, they may find that they've still been "getting" higher during those last 12minutes, they hit that hoots peak. That's why dosing can be tricky, because on top of this you need to factor metabolism, medical conditions, tolerance, etc. So don't shoot the messenger if ya get over or under cooked hahaha not to mention they don't look like legal market so I'm basing this suggestion pretending those are for certain 15mg/unit.

Love cbd, and it may in fact be better for someone. But you’ll still fail a drug test. Drug tests work on thresholds. So if you have above 55ppm thc in your blood you’ll fail. Doesn’t matter if it comes from cbd or a high potency statin. Cbd strains will absolutely make you piss hot.... That being said. Drug tests that rely on thresholds are easily beat using the dilution method. You’ll need 3 things. Water. Creatine mono hydrate and b vitamins. The idea is you “dilute” your pee by chugging enough water before you test. You’ll generally need a minimum of 24 hours for this method. Chug chug and chug some more water. Wake up and pee once or twice THEN take the test. Your first urination of the AM will be the most potent. You then take creatine mono hydrate about a hour before you go In. Take at least 10g but not much more than that. Then the b vitamin. Creatine mono hydrate is not absorbed by the body so you pee it out. If you do it right, You now have in essence a piss filled cup that’s really just water, creatine and b vitamin for color. The creatine is important because they weigh the specific gravity at labcorp. Everything will work. The temperature will be right because it came out of you, the weight will be right because of the creatine, the color will be right because of the b vitamin. This is how you pass drug tests on the fly. Note: labcorp and quests threshold is 55ppm. At home tests, the kind you pick up at cvs, threshold is 35 ppm. If you pass a at home test you’ll easily pass a lab one, the only exception to this is the military test they do when your going for a security clearance. That has a threshold of 15 ppm I believe. Shouldn’t really be smoking if you have a security clearance anyways. Good luck everyone.

Many people find CBD to be more relaxing than THC. While CBD is a cannabinoid like THC, it exerts its effects through different mechanisms of action. Research has shown that CBD can affect the serotonin system and the ECS in ways that may promote relaxation. For example, levels of the endocannabinoid AEA are correlated with feelings of relaxation, and CBD has been shown to elevate levels of the AEA by inhibiting the enzymes that break it down. You can incorporate CBD into your relaxation practice in lots of creative ways. For example, you could massage a CBD balm into your feet before taking a relaxing walk. Or you could take a CBD oil capsule before your meditation session. Or you could take a few puffs of a high CBD strain before taking a bath. Both full-spectrum CBD and isolate CBD bolster your endocannabinoid system and can promote feelings of relaxation. Start low and you can always add. Cannabis isn’t a cure all and talking to a therapist would be a great addition. 🌱💜

Most people don't value terpenes and those are the really difference makers on the desired effects, ence the difference between strains, even in THC rich cannabis, that's why you can find thousands and thousands of different strains. Terpenes are, in a very short version explanation, like essential oils that cannabis share with other flora type plants and fruits such as Rosemary, pines, origanno, strawberries, mangos and many others. It's sad that producers do not test terpenes levels on their flowers like some do in the US. Terpenes give the cannabis it's properties, whether it's a strain that makes you fuzzy, a strain that makes you sleep or that makes you laugh your ass off. You can have a strain that has 10% THC high in terpenes and a 20% THC low in terpenes and I can practically guarantee you that the lowest amount THC strain will probably give you better, stronger effects, besides the flavour. For anxiety, the best terpene is Limonene, which is also present in citrus fruits such as lemons, oranges, etc, and that's the one that could really help improve the fight against anxiety (ex.strains know THC/CBD- Lemon Haze, OG Kush). Linalool is also excellent choice for both anxiety and pain (Ex.strains - Blue Dream, Zkittlez) For joint pain, Humulene terpene is excellent, present in strains such as Girl Scout Cookies and White Widow I strongly recommend you to read about terpenes and their true power in cannabis. I’m in the middle of a long-ass process of teeth-pulling, abscess-healing, bone-grafting, titanium-screw-inserting, and eventually caps/crowns/something looking like real teeth on top. It’s fucking expensive, a fucking long process, and there is no way in hell that I would mess this up by deviating from my oral surgeon’s or dentist’s instructions by one millimeter. (I had my bone graft in November, and just got my titanium screws in mid-May, so I had to restart healing process all over again from scratch really recently.) I didn’t smoke/vape at all once I knew the surgical procedure was scheduled and upcoming, I switched to edibles with CBD in them as well. I’m probably rare, but since I had follow up appointments scheduled so frequently post-op each time, and never quite knew if I might get cleared for the next surgery sooner rather than later, or might need a revision of the bone graft, or might have to wait longer than we thought (because of slower than expected healing), it was just better all around to stay off vaping and stay on the edibles. I don’t entirely know why smoking and vaping are bad/worse post-op and while healing, when the same active ingredients so to speak are okay in edibles- maybe it’s literally just the smoke/vapor in the mouth interferes with the healing/microhealing happening in the mouth. Maybe it’s the sucking motion, similar to how straws are banned too. Maybe it’s both or something else or whatever. But whatever it is, nothing in the world could convince me to risk the situation, unless and until I got clearance to return to vaping/smoking. Maybe I’m doing better just sticking to edibles because I’m taking ones with 1:1 THC:CBD, having switched from vaping just hybrid flower. I don’t know why you’re having problem staying with edibles for another 10 days, and not being able to wait to get back to smoking? What is 10 days, compared to risking everything you’ve gone through so far, and the rest of the process that’s left (whatever it is that is planned out, if anything)? If you smoke and bled, then it was too soon to smoke, and should have been clear that your dentist/surgeon’s instructions are correct. If edibles are still too strong for you after however many days post-procedure you are (20 days?), then take smaller edibles? Cut them? Edibles come on slower and stay in your system longer, and so people used to smoking or vaping get used to the instant feel instant high, and get impatient with the time that edibles take to take effect. So, maybe start earlier in the day with a smaller dose, maybe? Give it more time to hit, and only after determining if it’s really not enough, decide to take more. And maybe switch to one that has some CBD as well? Just some things to think about, from someone who has been through the same procedures.

This will partially depend on what the carrier oil is; a polyunsaturated oil like hempseed oil will go rancid much sooner than a saturated oil like coconut MCT oil. When my CBD oil has gone rancid, it’s very obvious: it goes from tasting pretty mild to tasting horrible. However, some CBD oils taste pretty nasty even when they are fresh, so it’s a very subjective determination.

I recommend splitting that up into 10 equal pieces (so splitting into 10mg chunks), and only eating half of one of those tenths. If nothing happens or if the effect is too weak, eat the whole tenth. Rule with edibles: Always eat less than you think you can handle. You can always eat more.

Short answers - if you want to. Long answer - I'm 30Y/o right now, started at 14 as a wee little girl who knew nothing better. Dosing is one of the hot topics and challenges for the industry these days, especially with dosage recommends and potency going up. First off do what works or is working for you, but do not be shy of upping the dosage a bit even at your small frame. There is no set answer but understand net absorption rates which can vary from under 10% for some ingestibles to vaping up to 60%. If doing a traditional oil tincture, you're looking at 10-15% net mg absorption for comparison sake. You might find another method that nets you more and therefore more effective with doing less. Tinctures are not cheap (Lazarus one of the better values) but when you start having to double your dosage it gets pricey.

Full Spectrum is the stuff that contains THC. It's usually in small enough amounts that it doesn't make people high, but some people are more sensitive than others, so it's up to you whether or not you want to take that risk. Broad Spectrum is pretty good too - it contains supporting chemicals like other cannabinoids, terpenes, and flavonoids. Edibles can be easy to start out with, but difficult to dose more specifically. They're also filled with other ingredients and sugars, and might be more expensive in the long run. Oils are a bit easier to custom dose, and are a bit more bioavailable (more CBD is absorbed by your body). It kind of comes down to personal preference though. In terms of finding products, the best thing to look out for is a public certificate of analysis, or COA. These documents usually contain things like the product's chemical makeup and things like heavy metal and bacteria levels. This transparency is a pretty good thing since there are some sketchy products out there. Good luck! I hope you're able to find something that helps!

CBD is a hydrophobic molecule, so it repels water. When ingested, a very high percentage of the total amount of CBD is destroyed by your liver prior to absorption. Teas/edibles also typically come with a premium due to the convenience and novelty aspects, so while they are, technically, viable dosing methods, the high cost and low bioavailability are considerable drawbacks. I'd recommend using a tincture. The dosage flexibility and accuracy is incredibly useful, sublingual bioavailability is quite a bit higher than ingestion, and it's one of the most cost-effective methods of dosing. Regarding dosage, this varies wildly from person to person, so you'll just want to start low and increase gradually until you achieve desired results. What works for one person may be far too much/little for the next.

You can , but it's wasteful and ineffective. First off: CBD is a hydrophobic molecule, so it will not mix with water and instead will repel. This means the majority of CBD will cling to the surface of the tub, or float on the surface of the water. Second, transdermal absorption of CBD requires dwell time, and given that your body will be largely submerged in water you won't be achieving sufficient dwell time. Third, the average bath tub will hold 80 gallons of water with around 10 square feet of surface area, so as Khyron mentions there's a real issue with dilution. I'd strongly recommend putting that isolate into a balm or topical product that you can massage into your skin and leave there, so it's able to achieve proper dwell time.

Living in a legal state, the price of weed is regulated by the market. If your bud is overpriced, you likely won’t get many customers. If your bud is too low in price, you’ll go out of business. Some company’s are really good at marketing and advertising, so they can artificially inflate their price by making people think it’s of higher quality or that it’s trendy. For example, a tray by cookies is going to cost more than just a random regular tray, because you’re paying for the brand name as well. Some weed has higher thc levels some have more cbd, so there’s a wide variety of different products that all range in price based on, quality, branding and availability. And people who buy these products end up deciding the price based on how much they choose they will or won’t spend. As for black market stuff. That market is not regulated and purely dictated by the sellers. If you want something but have no other way of getting it, sellers generally charge more because they know you don’t have an alternative. If there are lots of sellers on the black market selling the same thing then prices may drop. It’s hard to tel with the black market. Im sorry if people live in areas where weed is illegal. I've been using CBD consistently for 6 years now, with virtually no breaks, so here's my quick weigh-in: >long term effects on memory and anxiety No effects to speak of, aside from the anxiety relief provided by dosing CBD. I continue to use THC products so my memory is affected, but I'm confident that's caused by the THC, rather than CBD. >bodily smell by daily consumption None to speak of, and I am (no idea why) unusually sweaty at times, so thank goodness lol. >The right form/dosage for CBD I primarily use full-spectrum tincture, but I keep edibles/capsules on-hand for dosing on-the-go, as well as distillate vape products and hemp flower. In terms of dosage, I'm using 150mg of a tincture with a fairly narrow cannabinoid profile, dosed twice per day. Appropriate dosage varies wildly, though, so I need to stress that my taking 150mg has no bearing on what dosage will work for you, and the cannabinoid profile of the specific product will affect this as well (along with terpenes, if present). On this topic you'll want to brush up on bioavailability, duration of effects, and onset of effects to decide which form is right for you. For example, the bioavailability (percentage of a substance that is absorbed by the body) of ingested CBD (swallowed straight - not to be confused with sublingual \[under the tongue\]) is significantly lower than that of inhalation. The duration of effects, however, is significantly longer when ingested. Anyway, that should get you started, but please feel free to let me know if you're wondering about anything else.

She’s referring to bioavailability of cbd, and is correct noting it’s not very efficient. Being a lipid/fat based extract, our gastro intestinal system does a very poor job of absorbing it into the blood system. (The over-simplification is that our bodies are made of water, and oil and water famously don’t mix, but it’s more complicated than that) Some studies have shown that cbd ingested as an edible (gummies or even oil dropped into a beverage) often gave a bioavailability as low as 4% though some studies have shown subtly higher This is why sublingual ingestion is best (it can be apsorbed into the bloodstream by passing the digestive system, for greater efficiency. I believe in the 30% efficiency range. Vaping and suppositories offer greater bioavailability but come with either health risks or… convenience concerns? Pro-nano-lipisode tech (nano cbd) was invented as a solution for this very problem

pre heat your oven to 250F on a baking sheet spread about 1 ounce of flower or 4 ounces of trim and lay a sheet of foil over it. place the baking tray of bud into the over for 30 mins. this process is called decarbing where we are getting the THC to activate. while this is going, get 16oz of olive oil or coconut oil and 2 cups of water and bring it to a light simmer in a small pot. VERY LIGHT SIMMER. once the buds are done, let them cool for 5 minutes before adding into the oil. now simmer the bud in the oil and water mixture for 4 - 5 hours stirring occasionally. keep the pot at a low bubble. after the time is up, strain the stuff through a wire mesh bowl thing (wholy bowl) cheese cloth might work as well. you can use this oil for baking, cooking or whatever. Some researchers believe that the properties of a chemical found in marijuana may help the brain to repair itself following a traumatic injury. The reason marijuana and concussions may have a beneficial relationship to one another is because cannabidiol (CBD), a chemical found in marijuana, might be able to help reduce inflammation in the brain following an injury. Cannabis plants have antioxidants that can provide protective features to the brain, and they could possibly reduce overall inflammation and stress-related damage in the brain. This is important because one of the primary impacts of concussion is neural inflammation. There was a Tel Aviv study showing that small doses of THC, which is the psychoactive component of marijuana, helped reduce swelling in the brains of animals. Yes, for decades. I think most people with as many anxiety issues as I have with cannabis would have given it up a long time ago. Here are some suggestions in no particular order: If you live in an area with legal cannabis or otherwise have access to a variety of strains, look for one with a ratio of THC:CBD of approximately 1:1. For example, if it has 9% THC, it should also have around 9% CBD. There's research behind this, but essentially the CBD mediates the impact of the THC and balances it out, providing a more "mellow" high. Don't overdo it. Get a vaporizer with fine temperature control and start at a low temperature, and ease yourself into it. Take 10 minute breaks during your session to evaluate where you're at and whether you want more. I use the Mighty+, but there are less expensive options available. Embrace it. As someone with an anxiety disorder, I've learned to use cannabis as a tool to improve my coping skills. Every once in a while, either on purpose or by accident, I will get too high and it will trigger anxiety like you're describing: paranoid/panicking thoughts, high heart rate, and general sense of dysphoria. I used to resist this and try to calm myself down. Just like with anxiety you experience when you're sober, resistance only makes it worse. Now I use these episodes as an opportunity to practice "riding" the anxiety. Be mindful of it, how it feels, what thoughts you're having, how your monkey brain wants to respond to it. Try to detach yourself from those thoughts and feelings and be a curious outside observer of the experience. I've found that doing this teaches me a lot about myself and my anxiety and "trains" my brain how to manage anxiety episodes in a more productive way. I've had wonderful insights into my personality and my anxiety disorder while stoned on cannabis, and these carry over into every day life.

I’ve had COVID 3 times. I’ll give you just a bit from my experiences (American). On my first go around (February 2020), I couldn’t get into a hospital. I was left to do the COVID thing on my own. Had a fever for several days on the initial ramp up. It started with a dry cough that turned into a deep cough. My lungs were in pain after day 5. Every breath hurt and this continued on for the next 2 weeks. Body aches were the worst I’ve had in life. Just felt like I had done a full body work out every day. I had heard that inflammation seemed to be the culprit of a lot of the deaths happening already. They couldn’t keep the inflammation down in the patients. I decided to try a CBD vape that I had because I thought direct application of an anti inflammatory might help (this was a scary choice to make, I’ll be honest. I was sure that I was gonna die anyways so I decided to try it). I also decided to try an Albuterol nebulizer (had one from my son when he was a baby). Albuterol is a bronchial steroid used to open up your airways. The CBD eased the pain in my chest. For the first time I could breath and it not feel like hot ashes in my chest. The Albuterol helped with the coughing. I continued to use both twice a day. 5 puffs on the vape, and the recommended usage of Albuterol. Within a week the pain subsided from my chest, cough lingered for an additional 4ish weeks. December 2020, I get COVID a second time (CBD didn’t prevent me from getting COVID in my experience). This time I was prepared. In the in between time inhad started making my own CBD edibles (120mg CBD, 10 CBG, 7 THC). I took one every day of my time with COVID. I never developed the serious cough from my first experience. I did still have an annoying dry cough, but a tremendous improvement. This bout with COVID left me exhausted. Body aches were present for the first full week. I couldn’t get enough sleep, but my overall symptoms were much milder. I was sick for 2 weeks. Did not have energy to even sit up some days. I lost my taste for 3 days during this experience. I got vaxxed May 2021, boosted Nov 2021 Third times a charm! Beginning of this month (August 2022), wasn’t feeling well. Figured I’d double check just to be sure, but I had COVID. I’ve been back on a smoke kick and I’ve been smoking probably an ounce a week of CBD bud (again, CBD in my experience, doesn’t not prevent COVID). I vaped the 3rd and 4th day of COVID, and then went right back to smoking. This go around I was out for exactly 7 days. Most days, I got up, moved around. Went outside to get sun. By the end, I’d have bursts of energy and clean things around the house and stuff but tried to take it easy. Cough was present but never developed past a dry cough. Body aches were minimal this go around as well. Because I’m vaxxed and boosted, it’s hard to say what CBD contributed to my most recent experience. My experience is completely anecdotal, but I did want to leave it here as a part of the discussion. Let’s break down usage while we’re at it: - Vape - 3mg per puff/as needed and no less than 5 puffs - Homemade Edible - 120mg per/once a day - Smoke - 510mg a day (3 King rolls on average a day @ 170mg CBD a roll)

Full spectrum means the oil extracted from the hemp plant contains the full array of naturally occurring cannabinoids, including THC. Full spectrum products often also contain more terpenes, flavonoids, and other phytomolecules than “isolate” products. That’s because when full spectrum extracts are made, processors don’t extract just one molecule, like CBD, from the oil. The amount of THC in full spectrum products can vary and be as high as 0.3% by weight. Note that some products can still deliver a couple mg of THC at this level. So if you don’t want to get high make sure you check the lab result (also known as certificate of analysis or CoA) for that product. You can also opt for “broad spectrum” products which are similar to full spectrum with the THC removed.

Weed is a drug that alters your state of mind and can both reduce as well as increase anxiety and this can change wildly between people or types of weed. I’ve found that for me personally, certain types of hash will trigger a massive anxiety response making my chest feel extremely tight and have difficulty breathing. Different weed strains can also cause differing effects, depending on thc/cbd levels and terpene profiles, and again the particular person smoking it. Easiest way to tell is to stop smoking for 2-4 weeks and see if the anxiety continues. If it does continue, then it might not be the weed (though it could still be exacerbating it). If it doesn’t continue, then it’s probably the weed and you might have to look into different strains, different ways of consuming it, or of course simply stopping altogether. At the end of the day, this is reddit and anyone can type whatever they think is right, if you’re having persistent anxiety issues and/or gastrointestinal issues, seeing your doctor is the reliable thing to do.

The How to: I rough chopped up the weed i wanted to use, can use shake or flower....doesnt matter. I put it in a mason jar and into my oven at 250degrees. After 30 mins, i take the jar out and open the lid to release pressure. If you do all the opening outside, your house wont smell of weed. Lid back on, shake it up, back in the oven for 30 mins. Once that time is up, open it up and add your oil. Weed to oil ratio will effect strength. My oil is for topical use only so i dont know the strength or percentage. Once the oil is in the jar, back in the oven. 30 mins then open it...back in the oven. After 30 mins, open and then i let it sit on my counter over night. In the morning, i strain it through cheesecloth. I like to squeeze the remnants and get every drop, people who eat this coconut oil say squeezing gives it a VERY strong weed taste. I like to put the oil in a squeeze bottle, like for catchup. Makes it easy to use. ENJOY!

Just don’t make the mistake of taking these numbers to heart. So like once you do go buy real edibles don’t think cuz you ate one of these 400mg bags that you can take 400mg of real edibles. Recipe for disaster. If they’re from Cali grey market, they’re likely around 1/10th the potency from what I’ve heard on average. But they just lay all the candy out and spray distillate so it can vary dramatically. If you didn’t know you can buy legitimate D9 THC edibles from hemp vendors now a days. They use the entire weight of the gummy and can add up to .3% THC by weight. So every 1gram of edible weight they can legally add 3mg of THC. There’s tons of 5-10-20mg gummies and such out there. Average price from what I’ve seen is around $20 for a 200mg pack of them. Which honestly I’d say would be well worth it if you’re buying these right now. Not throwing shade just being honest. Also it’s stupid easy to make edibles at home, likely much cheaper than even the hemp D9 gummies. Just take your bud, throw in a mason jar loosely tighten lid. Heat in oven at 220f for 25 min, add in coconut oil and sunflower lecithin(optional but recommended) at a 10:1 ratio, tighten lid throw back in the oven at 220f for 45 minutes, strain out the bud using an old cotton tshirt or cheese cloth and you’re good to go. If you get decent bud estimate it at 20% total THC aka 200mg per gram. If it’s a bit mid just say like 12% aka 120mg per gram. Unless you know the exact potency then obviously use that. But for most people this will be by far the cheapest way to go about it. Plus you’ll get a much better high than these distillate based sprayed candy edibles. The hemp gummies are usually pretty decent though, as they typically have a good bit of CBD and other minor cannabinoids which helps balance out the effects and gives a better more fulfilling high.

A faster heart rate benefits from dilated, not constricted, blood vessels. Your heart is trying to pump a river thru a babbling brook. I'm using a Geekvape m100. Just so you understand, I have just started doing this, so my method is likely to change as I'm a bit of a perfectionist. I have never used a vaporizer that wasn't for dry herb. This is a new investment, and I do like what I've done. Most importantly: it works. I have let a few people try it so there would be no bias. I make a tincture(master Wu's method. You can do a slow cooker method too, Wu's is just the fastest. Fuck waiting months for it), add it to some propylene glycol (I did a 60/40 mixture, respectively), heat it at a low temp (same you would use to make the tincture), and stir it up really good for about 10 minutes. Once I put it in a container I shake it some more for good measure. I use some 15ml dropper bottles and just use the dropper to put it in the chamber like regular e juice. Only thing I want to do is improve taste and strength, though neither were an issue, according to those who sampled it. Assuming you're doing a small batch of tincture and not reducing it to further improve potency, you'll end up with about 45-60 ml of tincture, which you would then add to some vegetable glycerin (all the information I saw said to use propylene glycol with the glycerin at like a 50/50 mix to preserve flavor, but I elected not to use it for personal reasons).

I would say CBD topicals help a lot with what I would call "chronic passive pain". What I mean by that is the "ache" (which can be quite painful for some things) you get from it, not the more sharp pain you get when you move in a way that makes it hurt more. It does slightly lower the peak active pain, but not a lot. If certain movements or positions give me a lot of pain, the topicals don't really help me much. But it very much helps me with the passive, lingering pain. Not completely eliminating it, but making it a lot more tolerable. Unless you have a specific need for THC—and it doesn't sound like you do—I'd stick with a typical full-spectrum CBD product, which contains a small bit of THC anyway. THC is indeed an anxiogenic for some, and it's intoxicating, so if all you're looking for is a functional anxiolytic then CBD is the way to go. To be clear I don't say that to villainize THC, just a matter of being purposeful with substances. As for dosage form, there are pros and cons to each one, and again we come to the whole "specific need" thing. Edibles are generally the most expensive method of dosing, and bioavailability is very low due to the majority of CBD being destroyed by your liver prior to dosing. Duration of effects is long, but if this is specifically "to decrease anxiety during meal times" then it doesn't sound like you need the long duration. Based on all the info provided I'd recommend a tincture instead.

This is something ive learned to do myself over the last few years. The oil at the top of your carts will collect in the seals and little nooks and crannies at the top. I used to use a lighter to melt down this oil in the past but that can burn carcinogens into your oil, which is no bueno. The absolute best way to smoke the most out of your oil carts is to put them in a plastic bag and submerge them in boiling water for a few seconds. This works with almost any cart, but there are a few notes to make; Both plastic and glass will work, but disposables run the risk of ruining the battery from the heat. Never do this to new carts or disposables that you are not absolutely desperate for. This is mainly for heating up the oil inside so that it can move into the cotton and be used. Some cartridges come with terps or other oils that stick to the surface or leave a layer at the top, giving it the illusion that theres still stuff inside. There isnt. If you cannot see the oil collecting after submersion for 10 seconds, then its probably not good oil. Also, since you are heating up the oil, it can leak, very easily. This is another reason to only use empty or close to empty carts and for short periods of time, as large amounts of oil can leak and get wasted if they get too hot. TLDR: Put them in a plastic bag and dip them in hot water.

There is a 2500 year old stash that was found in China that was all dense seedless buds that must have been carefully raised for smoking. All farmed crops get changed early on but remain closely related to their original forms. The change to extremely refined forms is a different stage altogether and really didn't start happening until much later. There is also the inherent problem that unlike other natural products like sugar or alcohol or cocaine cannabis is made up of many cannabinoids, flavanoids, and terpenes which makes refinement tricky even to define. So of course there are always issues with human progress whether it is industrial farming, GMOs, refining processes for seed oils or corn sugar or whatever else, but there are a bunch of levels to it and different stories and points of view, not just some simplistic "kind of a problem with fucking with nature" or whatever.

Here you go - 1 gram of cannabis - 1 tbsp of coconut oil or unsalted butter - 4 cups of water - A teabag of your choice - Your preferred sweetener - A strainer, coffee filter, or cheesecloth - A teapot, mug, and spoon Chop or slightly grind your cannabis, separating all the seeds and stems. Boil the water on a stovetop and add the coconut oil or butter, stirring vigorously until it dissolves. Add the teabag and your cannabis, lower the flame, and continue simmering for 7-10 minutes. Keep stirring every minute or so. A lower temperature will help preserve the medicinal properties of the plant. Remove the teapot from the stove, discard the teabag, and strain the tea slowly and carefully. If you prefer to use a metal strainer, make sure it’s a very fine one, or you’ll have bits of leaves in your mug. Discard any excess plant matter. Sweeten your tea to taste and add milk or lemon as you prefer. You can also opt to add a cinnamon stick or a spring of peppermint to the hot tea to mask the characteristic weed flavor. You can make weed tea even more easily if you have cannabis-infused butter, oil, or honey, or a cannabis tincture. Simply steep the cannabis extract in hot water with a teabag of your choice, mix thoroughly, add your preferred sweetener, and enjoy. Remember those cannabis stems you separated in the beginning? You can use them to brew weed stem tea and get a bit more out of your cannabis.

When you're smelling flower you are smelling a combination of terpenes (the profile) but as soon as you ignite that flower wether it be with fire or by heat or other heat source - you start losing terpenes. Some get burnt away because of the heat. Some get utilized. Terpenes are volatile compounds who degrade even at room temperature which is why proper storage is so important. THC starts to get released around 225°F but fire is much hotter than that. Terpenes start to evaporate/degrade at like 60°F. If you use a dry herb vape you'll notice a huge difference in flavor profile and can play with temperature settings to find your own sweet spot. I make my own wax and use heat to customize my terpene profiles to ones that are enjoyable to me.

There’s a lot to unpack for you here, boss. Setting aside the relationship issue (which I firmly believe must be solved before any kind of daily medication regimen becomes a part of your treatment) I think you should try marijuana partially set aside from the “treatment” element first. Get your card, head to the shop, buy a small multi-pack of short joints. Usually sold in half gram quantities per joint, this is a great way to start without much hassle or concern. Weed is strong, you don’t need to smoke it all in one go, and you can always have more later. Once you’re properly baked, go find your favorite album from your teenage or school years. I’m sure you have one that you know from front to back, all of the lyrics, every single note. Listen to that with some headphones and as few distractions as you can manage. You’ll almost certainly find something new. Do be sure to prepare yourself before you start. If you have concerns about being away from responsibilities for your family for 4-6+ hours, those will manifest themselves as paranoia and anxiety during your high. This is easily managed but for someone unaware of “how long will this last??” It can be mind-numbingly terrifying for a bit. Once you’ve had a few good sessions you’ll have a much better idea of potency you’re looking for and the dosage you think would be most effective for you. Edibles are quick, easy way to dose that can be extremely consistent, however they are relatively “slow” to work. This can be a bonus for your situation, I feel. Check out some of the low-dose gummies at the store, usually sold between 2-5mg/piece. Those are great for minor dosage in a consistent treatment regimen.

>Heating the oil is just to melt the cbd into it right? Yep! In particular, heating helps break down chunks of isolate powder. >Does it improve sublingual absorption? Does it make any difference when ingested? Heat won't change this, but sublingual > ingestion in terms of bioavailability. You might already know this, but ingesting (swallowing) CBD results in a large portion of it being destroyed by the liver prior to absorption, whereas sublingual allows you to absorb the CBD prior to ingestion. So to maximize you'd want to sublingual dose first, then swallow the oil.

On more than one occasion I've found myself inadvertently high off federally-legal hemp flower. I've been using high-THC cannabis from a dispensary almost daily for the past couple years, so I wouldn't at all say my tolerance is low. Granted, this will depend very much on the cannabinoid profile of the flower you're using, and how much you're using at a time. I'd strongly recommend sticking with broad-spectrum products overall, just to be safe. Broad-spectrum distillate can be bought very cheap and either vaporized or turned into a tincture/oil. There is no such thing as broad-spectrum hemp flower (yet).

TL;DR - skip to next paragraph to read my story LOL! . It appears that limited evidence suggests that CBD doesn’t necessarily have a solid impact on reducing the kick of the high. Actually, when the THC:CBD ratio is 5:1 - 3:1, it appears to slightly increase the intensity, even though it also tends to reduce the anxiety/paranoid/depersonalization effects a bit. In THC:CBD ratios around 2:1 - 1:2 it appears to smooth out the negative effects some, to include mental clarity, but doesn’t really impact intensity. Around the 1:4 - 1:5 range, it appears to strongly mitigate negative effects and provides some blunting effect on intensity, though it’s still pretty psychoactive at this point. This is also where it seems to start becoming slightly sedating. It really isn’t until we get to about 1:10 where it seems like the psychoactive intensity is significantly reduced (though obviously not completely thus the entourage effect) and the risk of the aforementioned negative effects is mostly mitigated, though at 10:1 most edibles will be moderately sedating, although this will depend on the amount of THC to boot. I question whether 25:1 is psychoactive at all and from what I can tell, even medical resources seem to consider this dose non-psychoactive, except in sensitive people who might still feel something. Sadly, research and even expert opinions on my comments seems limited, so others may draw different conclusions. Now for my story lol. So I feel very favorable to the THC:CBD ratio 1:4 and 1:5 gummies! Several things:. Vaping vs smoking is different on many levels. When you smoke- you are combusting plant material at a very high temperature. That high temp actually burns off many of the terpenes and other cannabinoids before they're useful in our lungs. Vaping allows you to bring more of the plants "entourage" effects into play - at lower temperatures. Depending on the strain and the temp you cook it at, can definitely bring out those qualities (sleepiness). Try vaping at lower temps (like in the 360 range and climb the temp per session to see what suits you). Good luck!

Stems do not contain more CBD than flower. The cannabinoid ratios stay exactly the same in all trichomes regardless of location on the plant, just some places have way more than others. So the concentrations throughout may change. I think the misconception that stems contain higher CBD stems from(pun intended) is due to the whole hemp rope is made from the fiber of cannabis plants, then people associate hemp with CBD, but that’s not the case. Hemp is just a legal definition to define cannabis by its THC concentration, nothing more. Anything below .3% total THC 30 days preharvest is considered hemp cannabis, anything over is considered marijuana. But that doesn’t mean all hemp has CBD or any real cannabinoid content either. I say all that to point out that if you have cannabinoids on your cannabis stems, they’ll be contained within tiny bulbous trichomes very small in size along the outside of the stem on what’s known as the bast fiber or the very outer skin of the cannabis stem. Those tiny 10-30micron trichomes will have the same cannabinoid ratio as the more densely populated, larger capitate-stalked trichomes on the calyx of the bud itself. So if the THC:CBD ratio of the trimmed flower is 80:1 THC:CBD, the cannabinoids you’d get from the trichome stems will also be around 80:1, that won’t vary a ton throughout the plant. Just the density of the trichomes changes which make some parts of the plant more potent than other parts. Then I’ll also add you need saturated fats regardless of the cannabinoid consumed in order to to get any meaningful amount into one’s system. This is due to cannabinoids being fat soluble. So the only way they get infested is if they’re taking with fats, and more preferably infused into the fats itself. So these fat soluble cannabinoids don’t get absorbed through our stomach and intestine lining like a water soluble drug would, they actually get sucked up by digestive lymph nodes which then send the fat and cannabinoids to the liver to be further processed. In the liver a step called first pass metabolism is then performed. The cannabinoids will be metabolized by a couple specific liver enzymes, and breakdown into a various metabolites. Then these metabolites are what enter our bloodstream. In the case of both THC and CBD very little actual D9 THC or CBD enter the bloodstream, most of what enters are these metabolites, some of which can be way more potent than the drug they started from as well. Like with D9 THC that mainly breaks down into a highly potent metabolite 11-hydroxy-THC, which is around 10x as psychoactive as D9 THC itself. That’s why edibles are a different beast for a lot of people. Sorry went on a bit of a tear there, but I see this mentioned almost anytime someone brings up this will stems get me high type posts. Always someone who mentions CBD, so I felt obligated to break it down and explain a bit. Then the other part, with the tea you’d need to at least add milk or butter to the water for anything to get into your system, or have other fatty foods in your stomach at the same time for there to be any benefit. Just trying to pass along some solid info is all. You can get high off stems, but best bet is to decarb and throw them in some fat, strain them out and do whatever. As for how potent are they, really depends on the strain, some produce more trichomes on the stalk than others, so you really need to guesstimate and try it as you go.

What a load of bullshit on their part! The law was probably intended to counteract THC concentrates, but the letter of the law catches gummies too if they are MJ derived. So 4 grams of THC itself can = a bunny with 0.3% THC by weight and they count it as x grams of THC…..so even 1 gummy, usually around 4 grams, is considered a felony. I think Texas is one of the few states this phenomenon happens in. My understanding is that police in certain towns/counties actually make seizing even personal use amounts of THC a high priority of the law! Ridiculous in this era, as even most conservative states consider THC simple possession a low enforcement priority. The excuse they use is drug cartels but given how much is grown and produced in the states, their THC/MJ laws are severely outdated. Not sure if they did already, but you’ll want to be sure the gummies were lab tested to show limited THC content (I assume 10mgs). Even MJ derived, 0.3% or less could be a mitigating factor and most 10mg gummies are around 0.2-0.3%. I hope this helps and good luck!

Well dang. If I could see the lab report on their website it would give some indication on THC content, but without that it's anyone's guess, unfortunately. Just as an example, I have one full-spectrum tincture that contains 2mg THC per 50mg CBD, whereas another contains 2mg THC per 200mg. Naturally, between the two, you'd be better off (in terms of likelihood of passing a drug test) using the second product. Although any full-spectrum product is likely to cause a positive test result for THC. Going forward, I'd strongly recommend buying only products that are third-party lab tested, with those lab tests made available to you. That's really the only way to know reliably exactly what the product contains, along with how much of each cannabinoid and terpene, and proof of having passed safety tests for heavy metals and pesticides. This info doesn't do you any good now, of course, but I think this is important advice across the board.

Every body is different. The liver can and does release bile, and in people with a gall bladder the bile goes into the gall bladder which controls when and how much bile is released. Depending on how your body compensates for not having a gall bladder, you may or may not notice many changes in how your body processes fatty foods or need to make dietary changes after your gall bladder is removed. So, not having a gall bladder doesn’t necessarily mean that edibles won’t affect you (and for those who are unaffected by traditional edibles, using “nano THC” or water-based THC edibles can be an effective workaround, as these are formulated to be immediately bioavailable on their own), but I have found that folks who are unaffected by traditional edibles usually have had their gall bladders removed. There can be other causes, but that is by far the most common correlation I’ve found.

1. When THC and CBD are consumed together, they can have a synergistic effect, meaning that the combination of the two can be more effective than either one alone. When THC and CBD are consumed together, they can enhance and modulate each other's effects. CBD can reduce some of the negative side effects associated with THC, such as anxiety, paranoia, and memory impairment. At the same time, THC can enhance the therapeutic effects of CBD, such as its ability to reduce pain and inflammation. Additionally, CBD can moderate the psychoactive effects of THC, making for a more balanced, less intense high. It's worth noting that the ratio of CBD and THC can greatly affect the experience you have when consuming the both together. Different strains will have different ratios, and everyone's body responds differently. 2. For the effectiveness of CBD for sleep and anxiety, CBD helps promote relaxation and reduce feelings of anxiety and stress, which in turn helps with sleep. So yes, it really does help. 3. As for the consumption method, both oils and vaping have their own benefits. CBD oil is generally considered to have a longer lasting effect, while vaping may provide more immediate relief. It can be a matter of personal preference and what works best for you.

I haven't had a lot of experience with CBD flower, so that might impact my thoughts. However, as someone who's struggled with a lot of anxiety, I find oils/capsules can really help me. Personally, CBD doesn't fix my anxiety, but taking a bit of CBD oil helps my mind calm down a little and things feel a lot less panicked. I've done a bunch of vaping, which I like because it hits faster, but the effects seem to go away quicker too. So oils, I find personally, are a bit more consistent. One thing to keep in mind if you're thinking about swapping is that there is a bit of difference in effect. Smoking/vaping kicks in a lot faster, though the effects can also wear off a bit quicker too. It's also more bioavailable (more is absorbed by your body), so you may find that you need to adjust your dose if you're trying out oils or capsules. There's also a difference between oils and capsules. Oils are generally sublingual, or get absorbed under the tongue. Which makes them kind of middle-of-the-road in terms of bioavailability, fast acting, and how long the effects last. Capsules are the same as edibles - so less fast acting and bioavailable. All that is to say, with oils/capsules, I find you have to do a bit more planning ahead and be a bit more consistent with doses. If you do want to try them out (and I say go for it), just be prepared to experiment a bit with dose, because it'll probably be different than smoking was for you. Also, taking high amounts of CBD is relatively safe - according to the science we have at the moment. So I might take oils to give me those more consistent effects but then vape a little to kind of supplement. What works for me might not work for you, but there's a lot you can kind of play around with. Good luck!

If you’re looking to avoid intaking THC, avoid smoking or vaping any CBD products. Small quantities of CBD get converted into THC via pyrolysis when it is heated up to be vapourised or smoked, and it does produce a psychoactive effect albeit milder than products with actual THC content. Broad spectrum should be fine if you’re taking it orally or sublingually, the trace amounts of THC are typically low enough that you shouldn’t notice any type of impairment unless you’re taking heroic megadoses. Full spectrum I can’t really speak about as I live in the UK and all our CBD extracts can’t contain any meaningful amount of THC. Not sure about the US and other countries where THC is looked upon more favourably by the law, you may find the levels can be high enough to have an effect, but generally if they’re listed as <0.1% or thereabouts, you shouldn’t have an issue.

I smoked habitually on a daily basis for roughly 15 years, I took college finals stoned, went to work high, drove around, helped friends move, exercised, helped my parents remodel a house they were looking to sell, etc. I smoked all day, every day & didn't let it get in my way until it was in the way & made the choice to put it down for what I thought would be temporary. I also experienced the anxiety / panic I'd heard so many people describe but never encountered myself before & that was very off-putting to say the least. My responsibilities in life also changed over time while I wasn't smoking. I started a family & have people relying on me to provide financially & medical benefits. Even though I'm in a state where cannabis is legal recreationally, I'm in an industry & at a position where I could be fired for partaking. Simply put, you will likely get high if you use a product that is 55% THC and 30% CBD or if it's the other way around (30% THC and 55% CBD). There is some research suggesting that CBD can "dampen" THC's psychoactive effects, and it is suggested that CBD could "block" THC's effects. Although the theory makes sense, there is no definitive evidence that CBD reduces, dampens, or blocks THC's psychoactivity in any way. Indeed, there is some evidence to suggest that CBD may enhance THC's psychoactivity, as it inhibits the degradation of the endogenous cannabinoid, anandamide. The evidence for both positions is intriguing but by no means definitive. There could even be individual differences in effects, as different people have different endocannabinoid tones. Route of administration and dose also needs to be considered, as cannabinoids have different effects at different doses (e.g. CBD can be stimulating in low doses or sedative in high doses). All-in-all, more research is needed in this area to get an idea of how THC and CBD interact together when in the human body. At the moment, the answer seems to be: "Research shows that CBD could reduce THC's psychoactivity, but it could also enhance it. This could depend on several factors, including dose, route of administration, and individual physiological differences."

If you took 20 mg....and it worked. Then 10 mg a few hours later .... obviously it's not going to feel the same because you're already activated your cannabinoid system. It's like drinking 6 shots - getting drunk then taking 2 shots a few hours later. You're already drunk. You won't necessarily feel drunk -er. If you took 20 mg today and it worked ....and 10 mg the next day...you may not feel the same not because of tolerance but because the dosage is different. And that's assuming you're using the exact same product from the same seller etc. There are thousands of factors that will effect how cannabis produces a noticable reaction. Product, dosage, weight, what you consumed and how, your headspace, even location etc. Your best rule of thumb is to mix up your usage regularly. Don't use the same product/dosage for an extended amount of time without taking a day off. And to use different options which there are many. Liquid THC, RSO, Gummies, Baked Goods, Drinks, dabs etc. You won't always get the same results every session so if it doesn't work- your body just might not be primed for a high that day.

Okay, so these aren’t CBD edibles. They’re basically just legal THC edibles. It says it was made with hemp, and could well be, as there are strains with high THCA. But they can still be made with solventless marijuana rosin, as a gram of marijuana rosin can have as high as 70-80% THCA with maybe like 2-6% CBDA. That’s why it says <LOQ which means it can be there but just not in amounts detected by the machine. This is also how it’s legal THC because the law says hemp products can’t have more than 0.3% total THC of the TOTAL WEIGHT of the product. Lol, bullshit loop, because you’re still consuming the same amount of THC regardless of the weight. The effects remain the same, except it’ll probably take longer to hit you if there’s more food to digest. Provincial - I'm assuming you're located in Canada, yeah? What province are you in? The regulations for buying cannabis products in Canada are super different from the States because it's regulated similarly to booze. Unlike the States, we have to buy everything from licensed shops and don't have access to things like third-party websites or sellers. Also, if you found the brownies helpful but not the edibles - dose might be an issue? Edibles are only really sold in 10mg increments, so it's possible the brownies had more CBD in them. There are also some not-so-great products out there too, which may have made a difference. If you're looking for pure CBD, look out for Isolate products. But there's something to be said about Broad or Full Spectrum products as well - which is something important to know about in case you haven't thought about that yet. TCH-P aka Tetrahydrocannabiphorol aka (-)-Trans-Δ9-tetrahydrocannabiphorol has far more endocannabinoid receptor activating bits on them, this makes it about 7-10 times as potent as normal Δ9 THC. The actual binding affinity is 33x higher but the actual psychedelic effect is 10x. It's also naturally occuring in some strains of sataiva plants and it's thought that some strains are lauded for their potency despite having the same or even lower percentages of Δ9 THC compared to other strains due to their THC-P content. It's found in the seeds of almost all strains of cannabis.

Repeated and frequent use. Edible tolerance goes up way quicker than smoking tolerance. When you smoke/vape, you are high for 2-3 hours, when you eat an edible, you are high for 4-8 hours, the longer you are high for, the faster your tolerance increases. Tinctures even moreso cause they get you high for 8-12 hours. Its also technically a different chemical, when you smoke, the THC molecule is absorbed by your lungs and then binds with the cb1 receptor, when you eat an edible, it is processed by your liver and it turns into 11-hydroxy THC, which is treated differently by the cb1 receptor. So you can have a high edible tolerance and a low smoking tolerance, or vice versa.

Marijuana is an amazing plant. Not just because it can help with so many maladies but also because of the safeguards that are built into it. One of these is the near impossibility to over dose and the other is the increased tolerance to THC over time as you use it. At some point your body is not capable of feeling it anymore. In turn you need to use more and more to get the same effect, especially where recreational use is concerned. This helps prevents long term over use and encourages users to take breaks in order to reset their bodies to accepting THC again. Eventually the user will get bored of the low effectiveness and high cost and stop using it. It is no indication of addiction as THC is not chemically addictive. It is merely habitual like phone. Even candy (sugar) is more chemically addictive than weed. While less dangerous than chemical addiction, it's still bothersome. You don't need to throw out your stash, just take a week off. Theraputic THC doses such as those found in RSO (Rick Simpson Oil) are quite high and require the user to gain increased toleration. The high strong buzz eventually becomes not a big deal, even at high doses and becomes very much therapeutic instead of recreational for a buzz and a good time. If you are concerned, perhaps find a different and more effective type and delivery method that works best for your requirements. RSO for example is best taken as a suppository and lasts much longer. Users often take large doses but not for a buzz. Maybe the doses required to maintain your therapy needs are too much to simply smoke? You may need to try edibles or extracts if the inhaling of smoke is either insufficient to give you effects or too much clorophyll laden buds to be burning all the time. Bottom line: You are very likely not addicted just habitual and have a higher theraputic need. No need to throw your stash away, just take a break and/or change method or dose to accommodate. I highly recommend walking, draining your batteries and stretching deeply before bed and when you wake. Honestly address your diet and activity. They are pivotal to your good sleep.. I'm a normal guy who's been safely at this for 35+ years. I also studied sleep professionally for nearly a decade. Take care. Hope this helps. Sleep well.

So I worked at a cannabis grow that had multiple phd peeps doing studies. Clones of a plant grown under as identical conditions as you can make it can have extremely varying levels of thc, cbd, terpenes, etc. This happened for all indicas, sativas, and hybrids. My take away from it is that labels and % mean nothing. The best way to know what’s in what you’re smoking is to lookup the lab result on the specific batch of oil you’re using (not possible with flower because 1 buds result’s will be used to label quite a few others). Then trial and error to see how certain high levels of different terpenes tend to make you feel, then continue buying oils that as closely matches your the ones you liked the most.

Anything that has a smell or taste has terpenes. It’s the chemical compounds that make up smells and tastes of everything. Every strain has a unique terpene profile, but many strains have a lot of the same terpenes in different concentrations. Limonene, Pinene, Myrcene, Linalool are a few of the main ones you will see. You can probably guess the flavors and smells of the first two by the name, Myrcene is in mangos and Linalool is in Lavender. There’s over 400 known terpenes in cannabis, but only a handful are ever in high enough concentration to impact the smell and flavor of the flower. Smoking a bowl, bong, joint are all going to burn the cannabis the same way and produce similar effects in varying intensities and durations. There are several extraction methods for making cartridges, some of which completely separate the THC from everything else , including terpenes. High THC distillate, which is the cheapest form of extract, may be upwards of 80% THC, and that’s because everything else has been stripped out so your literally left with the THC and excess lipids that can’t be removed. Some manufacturers will add terpenes back into distillate, either derived from the actual cannabis strain, or from other plants, which is where it gets shady since there’s no science on consuming non-cannabis derived terpenes. Other extractions like wax and shatter use a solvent like butane to do something similar, but it is not as effective at separation which still leaves other cannabinoids and terpenes in the final product. Live resin and live rosin both use the plant that is freshly frozen right after it is cut down to preserve it in a “live” state, which many believe preserves more terpenes and cannabinoids. Live rosin only uses water as a solvent, which is why some people will say live rosin is a “solvent-less” extract, as they don’t count water as a solvent. Because there are no other chemicals involved in this process, and the process of making live rosin is more natural, you can preserve the most terpenes and cannabinoids of any extraction method for what is called a “full-spectrum extract”, encompassing the full spectrum of the plants profile. This is also why rosin is the most expensive concentrate. Long story short, every extract is made differently and will produce different results. Ever strain is unique and will produce different results. Every smoking method is relatively similar, but will still produce slightly different results. For “body effects” like you want, you need to focus on CANNABINOIDS , not terpenes. And that’s a whole other can of worms. Edit: since everybody is asking about cannabinoids, I’ll admit up front I know less about them than terpenes. But that goes for pretty much everybody! There’s over 100 known cannabinoids in cannabis, the two most prevalent being THC and CBD. Other more common cannabinoids that you’ll see these days are CBG and CBN as well. You can probably guess from the name that cannabinoids are exclusive to cannabis, but that actually isn’t true either. Black pepper, Echinacea, and even Sunflowers have cannabinoids! Don’t ask me what they are cus idk lol. But, our brains have specific receptors designed to connect with cannabinoids when they are released in the body, which is why people believe that humans have had a symbiotic relationship with cannabis and cannabinoids for centuries. It’s literally part of us. Cannabinoids don’t impact flavor or smell, but more effects on the body. Yes, terpenes do also have effects that may be similar, but to a lesser extent. Everybody knows THC is the main cannabinoid, it gets you high, it’s the good stuff. CBD is most prevalent in hemp and exploded in 2018 after the federal government legalized hemp. “Legal hemp” is any part of the cannabis plant, seed or extract that contains less than .3% THC on a dry weight basis. Another can of worms there . People like CBD for its relaxing effects, it helps with muscle recovery, anxiety, etc. which is why it’s the most popular aside from THC. CBN and CBG can provide similar benefits to varying intensities. But like I said, we know even less about cannabinoids than terpenes, so there’s still a lot to learn. Also just a note to everyone thinking I’m some expert; I’ve been writing about cannabis since 2013, and I’ve worked in the legal industry since 2017. Everything I know I’ve learned from just doin some google searches and learning from my peers. Just because what I say may sound accurate (and I do believe it is!), doesn’t mean you should take it as gospel and move on. Do your own research, learn about the plant. I started as a basic consumer that just wanted to know what I was smokin on the black market. It evolved into a career for me. I’ve learned more about this plant than I ever thought was even possible, because I just wanted to know . The information is out there, you don’t even have to ask on Reddit. Be curious. You’ll find some shit that might flip your perception of cannabis on its head.

Like any CBD product, CBD creams and balms have the potential to interact with other medications. However, the risk of interaction is generally lower with topical CBD products compared to oral CBD products because the CBD is primarily absorbed through the skin and does not enter the bloodstream. That being said, it's still possible for topical CBD to interact with certain medications, especially if the medication is also applied topically to the skin. Additionally, some individuals may be more sensitive to CBD or have underlying health conditions that can increase the risk of interaction. As with any new medication or supplement, it's important to talk to a healthcare professional before using CBD cream or balm, especially if you're taking other medications or have underlying health conditions. They can help you determine whether CBD is safe for you to use and how it may interact with other medications you're taking.
