But know thy dosage^^ let's say you have about 15% THC in your flowers and You are going to make a tray of brownies let's say 24 brownies. A normal person will feel 5 mg, a regular smoker might want 20 mg an a full-blown pothead will want like 100 mg of THC. Do you want the plant parts in your cake? good idea with chocolate but not so smart in a vanilla creation, if not you math might be off a bit, because an simple extraction will never be complete, best to assume 80 % will be extracted. let's say we aim for 20 mg a pice, it's what I prefer, always warning consumers to try half a pice and wait like 30 min to one hour before eating more. 24 pieces a 20 mg is like 480 mg that's 0.48 grams, yes metric is the way, with 15 % contend we divide our 0.48 grams by 0.15 leaving us with a total of 3.2g needed for your cake. If you don't want to deal with the plant matter you further divine the 3.2g by 0.8 resulting in 4 grams needed. You definitely need to decarb first. Get a proper mason jar/lid (no plastic) so you can decarb in the same jar that you process the mixture in. My process for freezer tincture: Put Everclear in freezer. Decarb finely ground weed in jar with lid on. Let jar cool to room temp, then put it in the freezer for 12ish hours. Add the “frozen” Everclear to the jar until the bud is full covered plus another half inch. Less is better imo so your solution is more concentrated. Leave everything in the freezer for a week. Shake the jar 3 times a day. Strain through cheesecloth, strain through coffee filter, bottle into tincture droppers, enjoy your extremely efficient (there is no better way to extract the maximum amount of chemicals from flower) full-spectrum edibles.

Ime the negative effects, if you even still get them only smoking occasionally, are always temporary. When I cut back it was sort of random whether the effects were the same negative ones I got from over using (anxiety from hell, self criticism, etc), or whether I got that classic giggly high. I believe since cannabis can be considered partially psychedelic a lot can hinge on set and setting. Sitting alone in my room watching videos I seem to never have a bad time. But the other day when I vaped a ton before going into a huge comic con, I was paranoid and anxious for a few hours before I started enjoying myself. Which brings up another point, the experience can shift from bad to good within the same evening. The long term effects are due to returning to that same headspace every day and not giving your mind any time to clear from the fog. Try the weekend warrior route. Defamiliarizing yourself with that headspace makes for a more rewarding experience when you return to it, and something to look forward too during the week.

>Is a purely CBD strain something I should take in your opinion? No such thing exists. All hemp flower contains an array of cannabinoids and terpenes, including THC/THCa, so if you want "purely CBD" then you'll want an isolate product. That said, hemp flower is a perfectly viable option to try as a sleep aid. I've never found CBD or hemp flower to be very effective when compared to traditional aids such as magnesium l-threonate, [properly-dosed]( melatonin, valerian root, etc. I'm not sure that an inhalable is the best route for someone with sleep apnea, and the duration of effects is relatively short (1 hour) compared to a sublingual dosed oil or ingested edible, but seeing as you already have the stuff it's worth a shot. I just wouldn't expect too much, as that small bit of THC/THCa will inhibit REM sleep more per mg than CBD will encourage REM sleep. As flipper mentions, CBN is the sleep cannabinoid. CBD helps mainly if the difficulty sleeping is related to anxiety or inflammation.

First of all, a topical won't penetrate into your bloodstream. It's topical, which means it sits on the top of your dermis (skin) and will not penetrate through the layers to even reach the bloodstream. Tropicals are good for immediate relief for pain that is close to the skin. This includes muscle strains and areas of arthritis. The cream for your hands is fine, and you can apply as much as you want. The relief begins at 1-5 minutes and can last up to two hours. But since it's on your hands you will probably have to reapply often. You cannot overdose on a topical. If your pain is super severe, topical will give you instant relief, but won't last long so you'll be reapplying it a lot. Second of all, you didn't specify what kind of gummies you're taking but I'll assume it's also CBD. The gummies will take an hour or so to feel any positive effects but since you have to metabolize it, you lose some of the effect. That should last anywhere in your system as pain relief (like a Tylenol effect) for up to 4-6 hours. Depending on when you take the gummy, the effect may weer off before you notice it's done it's job (like at night). You cannot overdose on CBD. Let me repeat that. You cannot overdose on CBD. You might have a point where you take too much and you might feel agitated or restless (and in your case I'm only referring to the gummy, but you'd have to take SEVERAL gummies at once). Everyone's body is different but I guarantee if your pain is as bad as you're implying in the original post, you're most definitely not taking in enough CBD and not often to keep the pain relieved, or your CBD isn't reputable, or you're not giving it time to work. I suggest you go into a reputable CBD store and talk to the people that work there. A good store will have trained STAFF, and reputable product to be able to guide you through the multiple ways to take CBD, dosage guides, effects, etc. I work in one of those places. In my personal opinion, you should be looking into a Full Spectrum TINCTURE to add to your regimen. The tincture onset is faster (gets into bloodstream via sublingual), and more effective since you don't digest it like a gummy that loses bioavailability. The relief lasts the same (4-6 hours). Your local CBD shop will have a good variety of options: isolate, broad, full spectrum, strengths (the amount of MG in each bottle) and literature or education on all of their product. On top of the, Google is your friend and SO MUCH of the questions people have can be answered with simple searches, then you don't have to go into the CBD store blindly.

Tl;DR Labels use a lot of tricks (and sometimes outright exaggerate real results) to make weed look more potent than it actually is. In short for comparison, about .25g of this weed should be comparable to a 10mg edible. Generally weed labeled over 20 is going to be "good", higher numbers are generally stronger but not always. 15 to 20 expect like average 90s quality (not the best the 90s had to order, the average 8th kind of a thing). Any lower and it's for a light buzz or for people who smoke high volume with joints or blunts. The percentages are typically exaggerated, strong weed these days gets labeled around 30%, but in reality is probably more like 20%, labs tend to fluff it up. That's why some 21% strains feel as strong as 29% strains (Also there is a lot to discuss here about terpenes, but I'll leave it out). One thing to keep in mind about the percentage is it's by weight not volume. You hear 20-30% and you expect that 1/3 of the big nug is pure THC, but THC is denser than the leaf material, so if it's 20% they are saying you have .2 grams of THC per gram. However, they are typically listing THCA content, the acid form of THC, not THC (sometimes it's total cannabinoids, which would include CBD, CBN, CBG, etc, also sometimes in their acid forms). When THCA is converted to THC through heating (decarboxylation) it loses about 20% of its mass. Also, when smoking, you flash off a lot of THC as the plant burns (this is why a quality vaporizer makes your weed last 50%-100% longer, it is destroying less THC). For comparison, let's consider smoking .2 grams of this weed (and this is going to be some real real approximate numbers here). So we have 200 mg of material labeled at 28%, in reality it's probably 23% total that got inflated at the lab, and really more like 20% THCA. So we have .2 200 = 40 mg of THCA. As it's decarboxylated, it loses about 20% of it's mass so we are left with .8 40 = 32 mg THC. Now let's consider that we flash off a good proportion while smoking, somewhere between 25% and 50% (this is an estimate based on my experience using vaporizers and about how much longer my weed lasts, not super precise, but Im a nerd so I do weigh and track everything to compare). Let's say it loses 33% to split the difference. So now we are at .7 32 = 22.4 mg THC. When you consider this, at the end of the day what we actually take in would be closer to 10% in reality which is a more logical number. I think mg in edibles are probably the most understood, those products have consistency issues as well, but I think most people with access to legally produced edibles understand what 10mg feels like. So now someone may think, "Are you trying to tell me that .2 grams of that flower is supposed to be a 22mg edible?". Which yeah, sounds way off. However, when you eat cannabis your liver turns the THC into 11 Hydroxy THC that is somewhere around (don't quote me cause it's debated) 3 times stronger than regular THC. So for our comparison, let's reduce our 22.4 mg to about 7.5 mg of "edible THC". Now it's sounding better, that's saying about a quarter gram of smoked cannabis labeled at 28% is likely the equivalent of about 1 10mg edible. So point is, these labels can tricky. Should mention some are quite good and have much more granular info than others, including terpenes, THCA, CBD, CBN and the estimated true THC after decarboxylation. Some basically just give a total cannabinoid percentage. Depending on State/Province/Country you're in the rules for labeling will be different as well. I agree that you should keep the cart upright for purposes of keeping the wick wet. However, I disagree that this prevents clogging. First - my theory about what causes the clogging is this... The wick is usually touching the coil (some carts are different designs inside), but it is in very, very close proximity to the air passage. I believe that incomplete combustion and either the normal airflow, or more likely, drawing too hard, cause the oil to seep up the sides of the tube. As you hit it a few times, that oil is drawn away from the heating element and it cools faster than it reheats as it slides down the tube, accumulates, and then clogs. I do not believe the mechanism has anything to do with how the oil flows within the tube, and I suspect there is a slight negative pressure inside the oil chamber. FYI - you can clear a clogged cart almost immediately with something long and thin. I use the butt end of a burnt incense stick. Happens to be the perfect size and disposable. I thus do not believe that whether it is upright or on its side really matters. I've never noticed a difference and I go through a lot of carts - like I have 5 at various levels just in sight on my desk. The oil inside will have a varying viscosity, depending on the processing method. Even the lightest oils flow slowly though. But it is clear that it doesn't flow enough to pass through the wick on its own, or else all carts would come off upright shelf display clogged. That also means that being on its side isn't going to cause that to happen either. If anything, gravity assistance would make it more likely in an upright position. IMHO the best way to prevent clogging is to not draw too hard. Let the coil do the work for you and draw a nice thick cloud with slow airflow. You could also run something down the tube regularly.

If legalized the price drops dramatically over time, which is awesome for consumers-but not necessarily for tax revenue or growers. Here in neighboring Michigan the 5 nearby dispensaries to my home (all on 1 street!) have great cannabis for less than $50 an ounce. For perspective: In Michigan right now all adults can possess up to 2.5 ounces of cannabis on their person or in their car (10oz if locked in the trunk) up to 15 grams of extracts, grow 12 plants with no oversight or restrictions, and most former misdemeanors and felonies for cannabis are now civil infractions or not a crime at all. Proposition 1 is one of the worlds most liberal recreational cannabis legalization laws. There are recreational dispensaries as well as home cannabis delivery services pretty much everywhere here. All this has lead to a huge overabundance of supply resulting in the highest quality cannabis of 25-30% THC selling for $50 an ounce on the black market. Also the liquor stores and convenience stores as well as some gas stations sell marijuana product such as Delta 8 and HHC cannabis flower, pre rolls, and cartridges for vapes. Many low THC CBD products including all that and much more are also sold at many establishments. The overall effect is that profits for criminal enterprises and large commercial growers are on a massive decline which is great in my opinion.

Myrcene is the terpene that makes you sleepy/calm, so if that’s the dominant terpene it’s an indica. Linalool is going to be found in lavender. If you really want to geek out and get snobby with your flower, google different terpenes to figure out what they do. When I worked at a dispensary it was explained to me like this: THC is the car. It will get you high, no problem. But Terpenes are the driver to take you to a certain kind of high. They’ll direct how you’re feeling. That’s why when you get vape cartridges, the best thing is to get live resin or full spectrum, because it hasn’t extracted those terpenes. You can get strain specific with those.

Strains largely come down to varying cannabinoid and terpene profiles, so on that I'd recommend looking into what each major cannabinoid and terpene actually does, then seeking a strain that features a high amount of that cannabinoid/terpene. CBG is good for daytime use and focus/energy, CBN is good for nighttime use, etc. Linalool is a sedative and anxiolytic, humulene is a pain-reliever, terpinolene is an antimicrobial, etc. Lots to consider! One thing I'd mention with hemp flower in particular though is that the THC/THCa content \ can\ be enough to produce a bit of intoxication depending on your tolerance, amount of THC/THCa contained, and amount of flower used. It's wonderful stuff, but even as someone with a high THC tolerance I wouldn't smoke it before driving, for example. Unless you’re taking broad spectrum (no thc) cbd, then what ever you’re taking most likely has some thc in it that it causing you to feel this way. These days, for some products, cbd is some what of a dog whistle for low dose thc. This is a new revelation to most here and not everybody has clued in on it yet. As such, you’ll get responses from some who will unintentionally try to gas light you into thinking it’s you and not the product. Reactions to low dose thc are very subjective; each person can react differently. For instance, 2mg thc, for me, is enough to cause anxiety, whereas 10mg thc is a typical entry level for recreational doses. The sweet spot for me is 100mg cbd/1mg thc, enough to feel relaxed, calm my ibd, but not get me high or anxious. Some cbd products can have 1.5mg thc per dose; if I were to take 2 gummies with that, I would have to clean the demons away. If you can, find out the thc level in the product you took and look for something that has less.

Akira Botanicals has like one or two CBD carts, but most of theirs are definitely THC-analogue-added. Their Pinewalker is the bomb though. I know someone who has tried so hard to feel an edible, even eating 1000mgs while in colorado not smoking what so ever that day and still nothing. It all has to do with your genetics. Basically in our livers theres "P-450" enzymes, and the level of these enzymes determines the rate of drug metabolism. "Certain foods can affect how the P-450 enzymes metabolize drugs by slowing them or speeding them up, such as grapefruit being a significant culprit for severe adverse reactions and even overdoses P-450 enzymes metabolize THC, CBD, and other cannabinoids. Are you trying to actually extract hemp oil from the plant? Or make an oil Infused with cbd from hemp? Your post reads like the latter so we will go with that. You smoke about 4oz a week? That’s literally wild as fuck 😂 Legit not trying to be an ass, but you’d get so much higher if you practiced a bit of moderation. I’ve had all sorts of tolerance levels throughout my years of using, and by far the strongest best highs are always while I keep my tolerance low. After about the 2-3.5gram of flower a day range I’d always reach a point where I’m essentially not getting stoned. Or I’m high constantly, so high just feels normal. Which at that point all smoking more does is give me a little boost for 5-10min after smoking and I’m back down to the general kind of stoned feeling. There’s a reason for this though, when we use THC it fills our cannabinoid receptors causing the high, but they get down-regulated in the process aka “taken offline”. It then takes time for those same receptors to empty and up-regulate aka “UAV back online”. When we keep smoking while having a ton of receptors offline, our bodies start to produce more and more cannabinoid receptors so it has somewhere to put the additional cannabinoids being consumed. This is what tolerance is on a super basic level, it’s from having way more receptors to fill than a non user, and we have to fill them all to get the effects we are after. The good news is it doesn’t take long for our receptors to up-regulate. One study found that after just 24hours of cessation that the stoners had about 50-75% of the open receptors compared to the non using control group, and after just 72hours there was no statistical difference observed. This means a short 1-3 day break every so often would do wonders for a user looking to get stoned, and to help prevent tolerance increases. Of course, the longer the better, so a month break will help way more than a few days. It’s also better to use 1-3 times a day, and each session get throughly stoned vs sitting and smoking smaller amounts throughout the entire day. This is the reason carts can nuke someone’s tolerance quickly, as they lend themselves to constant microdosing, where the person takes a few hits every hour throughout the day or whatever. It’s also why the first smoke of the day smacks so hard compared to the rest, as you’ve given your receptors a solid 8hours to come back. So some basic guidlines and tips I’ve implemented that have helped insane amounts with tolerance and getting the best effects has been by taking a 1-3 day break twice a month. The next one is the 1-3 sessions a day, preferably with a good few hours between each. Another one has been using CBD in my weed. Which there’s a common misconception that any CBD at all will dull the effects of THC. In fact, that’s not the case at all, it’s only when you use a ratio of THC:CBD lower than 1:2 when you’ll even start feeling the CBD affecting the THC. Below a 1:2 ratio CBD has an additive effect, where the high is stronger vs THC alone. There’s multiply studies on this as well. The best effects were fined from the groups that used at around a 2:1 ratio THC:CBD. You can do this by buying CBD dominant hemp flower and mixing it in, or by buying mixed ratio strains. Many other ways as well. CBG has a greater effect at reducing the effects felt from THC, but with the added benefit of significantly slowing tolerance building. All cannabis has at least a little CBG, just like all cannabis has a little THC. So you’d need to add in some CBG dominant stuff into the mix if you wanted to utilize it for this tolerance slowing effect. Just careful with CBG, it stimulates adrenaline release and it’s a big part of the reason some weed feels so racy, especially bud that’s been chopped a bit early. Figured I’d share, as I’m sure many could benefit from this info. It is counterintuitive, you’d think the more you smoke the higher you’d get, but there’s a certain line for all of us that once we cross it, using more will actually make us less stoned overall. At a half Oz a day I’d have to imagine your big into blunts or fat joints, but even if that is the case it’s still well into the area of seeing these diminishing returns. Like I said, it’s happened for myself multiple times once I was in the 2-3.5g out of a bong range. Sorry for the long comment, hope this helps a bit, or at least gives some food for thought. Take care homie!!

Hi, I’m a budtender in Alaska and my shop sells both THC only edibles as well as THC:CBD 1:1 edibles. Basically, for just the psychoactive effects with the mild physical effects of the flower the THC only edibles are best. Indica edibles are good for sleep, even without CBD, and Sativa edibles are good for socializing and anxiety (in appropriate doses; high dose sativa anything may worsen anxiety.) Either way, with only THC the most effect a person will get is a potent head/body high. The 1:1 THC/CBD edibles are best for helping medicinally. A lot of veterans and older people with aches, pains, illnesses or injuries come specifically for those. The true effects of pain relief, sleep aid, anti anxiety, etc come from the combination of THC and CBD, so while the high might not be as potent as just THC, it is a well-rounded, longer lasting head/body high that is more medically beneficial than THC only edibles. If you’ve never tried 1:1 edibles before, I’d recommend trying them just to have an idea of the difference. You might find you prefer 1:1 over just THC, maybe the other way around. Either way, enjoy your weed and happy smoking!

I take smoking bud, not trim, no BS, good weed. I grind it up, maybe a half ounce at a time. I make a tinfoil envelope put the weed in and seal it tight by crimping the edges.I then wrap that in tin foil. I pre-heat the oven to 230-250F and decarb for a half hour to an hour with the envelop flat in the center of the oven. I remove the envelope from the oven and allow it too cool. I put the decarbed weed into a mason jar and cover it with 195 proof everclear, only a smidge more liquid than there is flower. If you are working with a small quantity like an 8th, use one of those wedding favor snap top glass jars(remove the rubber gasket) or a similar thin glass vessel with a lid so the alcohol to weed ratio is not dilute. I swirl it for maybe a minute maybe less and store it in a cool dark place safe from heat. Every 6 hours or so I swirl it around (not when I'm sleeping I just let it sit lol). After 24 hours I filter it through cheese cloth. I then squeeze the remaining cheese cloth with a handheld orange juicer/press thing for making orange juice. This gets mostly everything out. I then bottle up a dripper bottle, and store the rest in the freezer with a nice label. Should be close to 20-30 mL of tincture per 8th. This is full spectrum shit, practically rocket fuel for me. Think RSO, not shitty distillate tincture from dispensaries. 1mL is about \ 25-50mg(depending on the starting material) you honestly don't want to take that dose even if you're used to "50mg edibles". I recommend starting with 3-7 drops, and working your way up. Dripper bottles that have volume graduations are very handy here If that isn't concentrated enough I have tips, that kinda ruin the flavor a bit, but yea. I have some secrets to maintaining terpene profiles (to some extent), but I'm not ready to share those yet.

Electric vapes are easy to clean. I LOVED my Arizer Argo when I used it frequently. The glass stems have a "pre-portion" chamber which you can purchase a cap to halve the portion if you so desire. The stems click in and out super easy, and to clean just soak them for a little while in isopropyl and rinse with warm water. Occasionally take a Q tip with a slight dash of isopropyl and squeeze it out so there's absolutely 0 drippage and swab out any debris in the bottom where you insert the glass stem. I used mine religiously for about 2 years and needed to swab it maybe 2 or 3 times and I used that bad boy daily at least twice. As for use if you pack your portion full do not pack it tight, set the temp to 420F and let it "cook" for about 2-3 minutes and slowly sip on it. One sesh with 1 stem pack can last 10-15 minutes. The key is to set the right temp and SLOWLY SIP. It works off of contact heat and convection heat, so if you pull too hard you'll just cool it off and butcher your hits. Cannabis, a plant with a rich history of medicinal and recreational use, contains various chemical compounds that offer potential therapeutic benefits. However, when cannabis is ignited and smoked in a joint or blunt, the heat and combustion process can lead to the destruction or alteration of certain beneficial components. In this essay, we will explore the potential degradation of beneficial parts of cannabis when it is lit up in a joint or blunt. Terpenes are aromatic compounds found in cannabis that not only contribute to its distinct scent and flavor but also possess potential therapeutic properties. However, when cannabis is lit up, the high temperatures can cause the degradation or evaporation of these volatile compounds. This loss of terpenes not only impacts the overall sensory experience but also potentially reduces the entourage effect—the synergistic interaction between cannabinoids and terpenes that may enhance therapeutic benefits. The combustion process involved in smoking cannabis can lead to the formation of harmful byproducts. When cannabis burns, it produces toxins such as tar, carbon monoxide, and other carcinogenic compounds found in tobacco smoke. These substances can have detrimental effects on the respiratory system, increasing the risk of respiratory issues and long-term health problems.Smoking cannabis in a joint or blunt may not be the most efficient method of consumption. When cannabis is burned, much of the active compounds are lost through smoke that is either not inhaled or wasted between puffs. This inefficient delivery system reduces the bioavailability of cannabinoids and other beneficial components, making it less effective compared to alternative consumption methods like vaporization or edibles.To maximize the therapeutic potential of cannabis, alternative consumption methods can be considered. Vaporization, for instance, heats cannabis at lower temperatures, preserving more cannabinoids and terpenes while minimizing the production of harmful byproducts. Edibles, tinctures, and topicals provide another option for consuming cannabis without subjecting it to combustionWhile cannabis consumption through smoking joints or blunts remains popular, it is important to recognize the potential drawbacks associated with this method. The high temperatures and combustion process can result in the destruction or alteration of beneficial components, including cannabinoids and terpenes, while introducing harmful byproducts. Exploring alternative consumption methods that prioritize the preservation of these valuable compounds can help individuals harness the full potential of cannabis while minimizing potential risks to their health.

I’m speaking of the general mass that takes edibles from the ones that .5mg can get them a nice buzz to the people like me that can eat 4g of RSO and still not have a good time with it. THC is converted to 11-oh-THC through the metabolic process by the Cytochrome P2C9 enzyme. Some people just break down THC too fast to really have it convert over 11-oh-THC, and is referred to as “the bummer gene”, which performing copy you get depends on genetic variability. Some people literally just can’t get high off eating THC and it has nothing to do with tolerance. Some people will never get past not just getting absolutely ripped off super small amounts. Now the fun thing about all of this is that actual tincture(THC+solvent, not THC+carrier) will deliver THC through your lymphatic system that crosses the blood brain barrier incredibly fast and completely bypasses your first pass metabolism. Mixing Everclear and decarb’d cannabis using a quick heat extraction of THC from plant into ethanol and you have some really strong shit. So if you don’t mind drinking small(1-5mL) of Everclear? Then tincture is for you. Edit: I re-read what you said and I’d always recommend RSO or homemade edibles to you. That’ll be the cheapest way you could consume edibles that would work for you. I asked bing if you were right as I am too lazy this morning and didn't feel I needed a degree to differentiate between hemp based delta9 and marijuana based delta9 gummies... Here's what big had to say; Hello, this is Bing. I'm glad you asked me about this topic, as it is very interesting and important to understand. 😊 The article you mentioned claims that over 75% of delta-9 hemp products are illegally modified, meaning that they contain more than 0.3% of delta-9 THC, which is the legal limit for hemp-derived products in the United States. The article cites a study by the University of California, Davis, that tested 113 samples of delta-9 hemp products from various sources and found that 86 of them had more than 0.3% of delta-9 THC. The guy on Reddit who commented on the article seems to doubt the validity of the study and challenges anyone to show him how the delta-9 THC from hemp can be differentiated from marijuana in edibles. He implies that the study is a complete fabrication and that there is no way to tell the difference between hemp and marijuana based on their THC content. However, his comment is not correct, as there are scientific methods to test the THC levels in edibles and to differentiate between hemp-derived and marijuana-derived delta-9 THC. One of these methods is called high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), which is a technique that separates and analyzes the chemical components of a sample⁶. HPLC can measure the amount and type of THC in edibles by comparing them to known standards and using a detector that measures the absorbance of light by the molecules⁷. Another method is called gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS), which is a technique that vaporizes and ionizes the sample and then separates and identifies the molecules based on their mass-to-charge ratio. GC-MS can also measure the amount and type of THC in edibles by comparing them to known standards and using a detector that measures the abundance of ions. Both HPLC and GC-MS can differentiate between hemp-derived and marijuana-derived delta-9 THC by looking at the ratio of delta-9 THC to other cannabinoids, such as CBD, CBG, CBN, and THCV. Hemp plants usually have higher ratios of CBD to THC, while marijuana plants usually have higher ratios of THC to CBD. Therefore, by analyzing the cannabinoid profile of an edible sample, one can determine whether it came from hemp or marijuana. These methods are not easy or cheap to perform, and they require specialized equipment and trained personnel. However, they are reliable and accurate ways to test the THC levels in edibles and to differentiate between hemp-derived and marijuana-derived delta-9 THC. Therefore, the guy on Reddit who commented on the article was wrong to dismiss the study as a complete fabrication, as there is scientific evidence to support its findings. This right fucking here. Weed isn’t worth getting your kids taken away. Imma say something that’s gunna make me an awful fucking parent and to this day I still feel really shitty and guilty about it. But I want others to take this as a sign that if you aren’t being responsible with your stash around your kids, you better start. Because if you’re not, it’s not a matter of IF but WHEN this could happen to you. I live in Florida, a med only state. I buy BM, THCA and occasionally dabble in D8 edibles. I kept my edibles in my top dresser drawer thinking the kids wouldn’t go near their parents underwear drawer. My (at the time) 2 year old step daughter somehow managed to grab said edibles and completely eat the entire thing (tbh it was roughly 800mg). No one ever noticed until around midnight when she woke up crying. This crying though was a cry I’ve never heard a child do. It’s hard to explain the sound but it literally sounded like a baby zombie crying. She was cold as fuck but drenched in sweat. Her eyes were rolled to the back of her head and she was completely unresponsive. Kept nodding off. It was really fucking scary. I get that you can’t OD on weed but this literally brought my whole world crumbling down. I was hella paranoid considering she weighed roughly around the same amount as my then 5 month old son. She was literally around 20 pounds and such a tiny child. She wouldn’t eat or drink anything. She just laid in my wife’s arms literally looking like she OD on something much harder than weed and that it was slowly killing her. We called the ambulance, rushed her to the ER and I explained everything to the nurses. At first they thought she was just having night terrors even after I told them I found the empty wrapper by her bed. It took 48 hours of being in the hospital with an IV in her arm before she was no longer shakey, wobbly and 100% coherent. It took roughly 24 hours before she could even stand on her own feet again but couldn’t walk without assistance. I now keep everything under lock and key and I no longer keep a stash of edibles. If I want one, I’ll go buy it and take it right then and there. Other than that it’s strictly flower. I got some concentrates coming and those will definitely be staying in my “drug safe” Edit: I forgot to add. The hospital turned me into child services and launched an entire investigation. Me and my wife were both “interviewed” by the case worker and drug tested before we were able to get discharged. They then visited my house while we were at the hospital and took pictures of every nook and cranny in my house. They did that 3-4 more times throughout a 3 month period. I was given what is essentially a “babysitter” whose sole purpose was to make sure I wasn’t under any influence when I was around my kids. And because I mentioned that I have depression they sent me off to do therapy on top of regular visits to the mental health facility in town (each visit required a drug test)

That brand offers their capsules made with either RSO or Distillate. This is the Distillate version. So, the oil used to make it has been distilled, like you'd distill water, or, say vodka. That process purifies the oil in a sense, so you'll be left with primarily pure cannabinoids. The effects tend to come on strong and are often described as a "clean high" (think: comparing a triple distilled vodka shot to drinking a dark beer). The dark beer here is RSO. It is raw, mostly unprocessed cannabis oil, so it has more plant in it. You'll usually hear that called "full spectrum" which basically means that it has most of the terpines in tact along with other plant material. That adds more depth to the effects. If you're using it medicinally, you TYPICALLY want RSO, as it offers more of what the strain has to offer. Some people just prefer the distillate, even as medicine because it gives them the reliving feeling they like more. I'd recommend trying one version for a few days then the other to see what you think works best for you.

Today's review is a great strain from Apollo called Gumbo. It's got a great spacey and euphoric feel to it while still being very relaxing and indica leaning and smells and tastes pretty good to boot. Let's get into the full review. Packaging - The flower came in a small glass jar that was actually meant for gummies since they ran out of jars with the right label, but they did label the lid with a sticker to let me know. I don't really care what the label looks like personally, I just appreciate that it came in a glass jar, which I always prefer over a plastic jar or bag. The jar fits the bud well and is something I can reuse for other bud in the future as well. Appearance/Feel - My 3.5g weighed out to 4.4g, so almost a whole gram overweight, which is pretty awesome. The buds are very pretty and are a decent size. My 4.4g consisted of 6 buds. The trim job is pretty good. There are a few stems that could have been cropped a little shorter, but not bad especially considering that it was so much overweight. They are a beautiful mix of colors with some greens, plenty of dark purples, some oranges, and a very frosty covering of trichomes over everything giving it a fuzzy white look as well. The buds are fairly dense and seem to have a good cure. On first inspection it felt a little dry, but after breaking some buds apart and grinding it up it has more moisture than I thought. They snap nicely when breaking apart and are fairly sticky when grinding up and made a nice fluffy grind with a good moisture content that worked well for smoking and vaping. Smell - The smell on this one is quite nice provided you like that good ol funk. It's pretty strong and has a little fruit to it, but leans more earthy and skunky with a little pine and almost a sour hint to it. It's pretty savory and pungent and fills the room pretty quickly once you pop open that jar. Taste - I tried this through my dry herb vaporizer (Airvape Legacy Pro) and also rolled in a joint. When vaped the flavor came through really well. It was similar to the smell and pretty strong. I got plenty of vapor throughout a 5 minute session that was thick and smooth. When smoked it was nice and smooth and almost creamy tasting. It had some of that savory funk, a touch of sour, and just a little fruit in there. It smoked well with very little coughing and the ash was mostly white. Effects - I don't know the THC percentage on this one, but whatever it is the effects are strong. It smacked me around harder than I thought it would. The first time I tried it was a decent sized joint and by the time I was halfway through with it I was pretty well on the moon. It felt just as potent vaped too. It has a very nice euphoric feel that's pretty spacey and tingly. One of those strains you smoke and when it hits you you just say, "Damn that's good!". I did get some pretty intense dry mouth with it and the munchies hit pretty hard. It definitely enhanced touch sensations, the shower felt extra hot and times with the wifey felt extra good if you're picking up what I'm putting down. I didn't get any racing thoughts or heart or any real paranoia or anxiety I sometimes get from strong strains (sativa in particular), and it was relaxing and ended up making me feel pretty drowsy. Time did feel like it slowed down some too. Effects lasted a good hour and a half to two hours I'd say and I slept really well. Pretty top notch effects in my opinion, right in line with what I love about cannabis. Conclusion - This turned out to be a pretty awesome strain that has just about everything I like. The coloration is beautiful and it's super frosty. The cure was pretty spot on. The smell and taste is strong with a classic savory funk to it. The effects are relaxing while still being very strong, tingly, and euphoric. Just make sure you are ready for some dry mouth, munchies, and to feel pretty sedated.

Sorry that you’re dealing with derealization. That is definitely a challenge, especially when trying to live a normal and productive life. There are three types of CBD: Full Spectrum (up to .3% THC), Broad Spectrum (Negligible THC), and Isolate (close to zero THC). I usually recommend that people start with the isolate and work their way up to full spectrum, based on their needs. For you, since you’re goal is to minimize your derealization episodes, I recommend that you start with a CBD isolate gummy. It’s easy and convenient, and you can pop one or two of them whenever you need. If you need something stronger, a CBD Broad Spectrum Tincture would be appropriate, as it will deliver more CBD and some other compounds that can allow a more holistic effect (also called the Entourage Effect). I would stay away from Full Spectrum initially, as the small amount of THC can be enough to trigger derealization. It’s unlikely, but just to be on the safe side, I would explore that last. The dose of the substance must be progressively increased to maintain the desired effect. 2. Serious withdrawal symptoms occur when the use of the substance is suddenly stopped. Neither of these principles is applicable to cannabis. (1) Regular cannabis users like myself almost always settle into a consistent dose, and the symptoms of suddenly stoping are usually limited to lack of appetite and irritability.(2) The evidence has been clear for years. That’s why the government commissions from the East India Report, to the Canal Zone Study to the LaGuardia Report all came to the same conclusion - cannabis is not addictive. Prohibitionists since Harry Anslinger have advanced the gateway theory: that cannabis should remain illegal because it leads to the use of additive drugs like heroin. Common sense alone tells us why they are wrong. 100,000 heroin addicts in the United States and around 85 million Americans have used cannabis. The truth is cannabis is a gateway out of addiction, not a gateway in. Sources: 1. Hal Arkowitz and Scott O. What you're looking for is a good mix between THC percentage and terpenes. I'm going to go over some common misconceptions that people talk about all the time but are false 1. Higher THC means better Higher THC doesn't necessarily mean better, In order for marijuana to work THC needs to bind with your CBD and THC receptors, but your high is also heavily influenced by the terpenes that are in the plant. A good similar way of looking at this is aromatherapy on a higher more effective scale. More THC percentage doesn't always mean more potent, look for Bud or concentrate that is testing in the 3-5% range in terpenes, you'll notice their THC amounts are significantly lower but they will still kick your ass 2. Purple means it's really good. The amount or color of purple on the bud does not translate to quality. Most people don't know this but growing bud in a colder climate gives it this purple color. This has nothing to do with THC on the surface level. 3. Pricey means worth it I recently went to a dispensary where they were charging $35 an eighth for some white runtz and it was dry and harsher than an 80's alcoholic step dad. And most dispensaries you are allowed to look smell and sometimes touch which you are buying b courteous of how clean your hands are, try to waft instead of holding your entire face over the jar, and most importantly if they don't let you see it before you buy it, DO NOT shop there. MY FINAL CONSENSUS IS do your own research, look at Bud very closely and carefully determine if the terpene percentage is what you want, also you can determine how good the terpenes are with the smell. looking at the terpene chart is also a good idea of knowing what your bud is going to do to you, I recommend going to dispensaries with the most information readily available to you, that usually means that they grow in a more professional way and they are more organized. I've walked into dispensaries that have charged out the ass for bud that you can see growing from the lobby, I've gotten home broken it up and it's falling apart in my hands like dust, I've had concentrates be the wrong consistency, and I've had stuff not hit me when it's testing 28. Just do your research.

Any lithium cobalt battery can explode. A number of things have to happen though - first the protection circuits have to die - these monitor overheating and overvoltage in any good quality device. Secondly it has to overheat - this can be caused by something like being left in the sun in a hot car, or by charging with the incorrect voltage from a dirty power source or bad charger. Sometimes heavy use, whilst charging can qualify. So there's a number of things you can obviously do to prevent this - buy well known, good quality brands likely to have good protection circuits. Avoid charging whilst in use, and charge in a good quality charging point, and avoid leaving it in very hot environments. In general this sort of event is VERY uncommon, but probably made more common by people having no idea how to care for their laptops, phones and vapes.

The first thing I'd do would be look into the licensing process for your state. Also, are there licenses available? It’s all about moderation and the plants make up. Marijuana needs to be further studied. To dig deeper, check out how terpenoids and terpenes affect the make up of plant strains, sativa and indica. Which most people generalize the two into categories but the specific bud names matter if you can test the plants make up. To quickly summarize terpenes are the genetic make up of a plant; what makes the plant smell a certain way, look a certain color, like purple, light green, or orange “hairs”, and how they feel sticky like super glue, or mids with no crystals under a light. Also the fresher the buds the more potent the effects the terpenes will have. That why people keep there stuff sealed. It’s kind of similar to when potato chips go stale after a day if the bags left open and don’t taste as good or when medications are expired yet still effective, just not as potent. Yes, you will experience different highs based on method of consumption. Smoking give you an immediate high, and vaping does as well, but vaping produces a much cleaner "high". This is due to the fact that you are not combusting (lighting aflame) the flower, rather you are heating it and inhaling the pure vapors- there's no smoke involved. In the case of vaping- whether it is liquid or dry herb- because there's no smoke and it is only pure vapor, many tend tonexperience not only a cleaner high, but a more intense one. As for edibles, yes, it does have a different effect because instead of giving an immediate high as with smoking or vaping, you won't feel any effects for at least 30 minutes to up to 2 hours because your body has to digest the product first before any THC gets into your bloodstream. It is a much slower process but produces one of the most powerful highs. If you've never tried edibles before and want to, be warned: start low and slow. Everybody (including myself) has the same experience: "I tried some and didn't feel anything after a few minutes so I tried more. Still didn't feel high so I ate more. Still nothing so I tried more... but then it hit after about an hour and I fel like I was going crazy/going to die". Peoples experiences all tend to be some variation on this theme. They took too much and then felt crappy. Now, we all know weed can't kill you, so you aren't going to die, but it can make you incredibly uncomfortable for several hours, maybe even a couple days depending on your experience level, weight, etc. So always, always, always, whe doing edibles, don't just chow down on an entire brownie or bag of gummies. Break off little pieces, then give it an hour and see how you feel. If you feel nothing, try a little more. But just remember, this is going to produce a different high vs smoking or vaping because it's going to enter your bloodstream vs going straight to your brain. All in all, just be careful. If you do accidentally have too much edibles, try to counteract with some CBD and that may help you recover a little fast, but ultimately you just have to wait it out. It'll eventually work its way out of your system. Have fun!

An experiment would be illuminating because I cannot find any literature about the volume expansion or contraction of CBD in MCT. If you think it's so simple, test your hypothesis with sugar and water. Sugar does not add volume based on its solid density. If it did, every gram of sugar would add .5 to .8 ml to its solvent, and that is not the case. 1 gram of solid CBD having a volume of 1 ml does not mean that one gram of CBD will add 1ml of volume to 30 ml of MCT oil. Mixing two compounds immediately adds another factor to how it behaves. It may add a significant amount of volume, an experiment would be needed to determine how much, but not 1 ml. Fundamentally, at least some portion of the CBD would be occupying a void space between molecules of MCT, and the volume of that void space contributes to the volume of the solvent, regardless if it's occupied by a solute or not. Solid volume is not the same as liquid volume. Liquid volume has more empty space than solid volume. This is the space that solutes occupy in a liquid, and the volume expansion or contraction is based on the intermolecular interactions between the solute and solvent and how those interactions improve or impair tight packing. Unless you do the experiment yourself and show that CBD behaves unlike nearly every other solute in a like solvent, I'm going to believe the theory behind how every solid-liquid dilution in chemistry is prepared.

I went through the same cycles of depression, short temper, and so on, but eventually that mellowed out. If you smoke weed and a lot of it, for a long time, your brain naturally produces less dopamine since weed does it for you. It's taken me YEARS to go from full dependence to only a handful of hits a week. After long-term, heavy usage, anyone will become dependent on it. Weed is definitely better than alcohol for long-term dependency, but clearly, he has an addictive personality that wasn't fixed going from one substance to another. His first goal should be to simply smoke less every day and do so gradually. Stretch it out over a year. His priority should be to go down to a bowl a day or less. Then, t breaks become much more doable. CBD is nice for helping mellow him out, but it isn’t going to be life changing. If he can reach the first goal, then the next year, he should try to go down to one bowl every other day, then once every two days. To dig into this a little for you, Do all edibles have the same high, nope absolutely not but based off what your describing you probably have distillate edibles, this holds true for cartridges as well Types of edibles: Distilate Full spectrum Cannabinoid blends Distillate: This is just thc isolated out of the source plant, many distilate gummies won't declare themselves sativa or indica because when you take just the THC you leave behind the other cannabinoids and terpenes, just thc isolated from an indica is about the same as just THC isolated from a sativa, so they'll say sativa or indica but you're taking the same thing and they're misleading in that way. If you wanted to get strain effects or like an indica or sativa effect you need full spectrum and not distilate. The pro of distilate is that it's cheap, the con is that the high isn't as nice and they're all the same effect wise and a lot less medicinal Full Spectrum: These guys have the THC from distilate and then also the other cannabinoids and terpenes from the cannabis plant, when you smoke bud those are the three parts of the plant that give you your specific type of high (check out kaleidoscope effect or entourage effect) full spectrum gummies have all three parts so they feel more like flower and you will see indica vs sativa differences. Cannabinoid blends: You can get these with either distilate or full spectrum but your essentially taking the product above and adding a bunch of another cannabinoid most commonly CBD but you can find blends with CBN for sleep aids and some other rarer cannabinoids (wyld has CBG gummies for example)

You've experienced what everyone who uses weed at for some time has- greenout. No, you've not broken your weed usage. You went too hard or got the wrong strain. Or both. Stick to the lowest dosage that gives you the feeling you want. Build up to it slowly. If you don't know the effect a strain has on you, start REAL low and build up. Try and stick with the same usage type- pen, vape, blunt whatever- so that you can figure out how each affects you. Blunts are harsh and fast, vapes are less harsh and slower etc. If you want to experiment, do so in a safe place, either alone or with people you trust, so if you do greenout you have support or don't get even more triggered by either being alone or with people who will freak you out. Most of all- weed is a drug. Like all drugs, your body has a safe dosage. With weed, that dose changes over time due to tolerance and won't kill you if you 'overdose' but you'll have a REAL bad time if you go over it and it's the wrong strain/situation/mood. Weed amplifies mood, so if you smoke feeling anxious, it'll make it worse. This comes from someone who uses weed to treat anxiety- yes, it makes it worse if you mis-dose. Same with depression. It can help, but get the dose wrong and it'll make it worse. So just don't go nuts and stick with strains that make you feel how you want.

From: How is Weed Tea Made? Cannabis tea can be made from any part of the plant that contains the cannabinoids you would like to have in your mug. Stems, kief, hash, trim, or shake can all be used, but starting with flower is most common. Today we will be using a gram of Death Rattle grown by Redbud Roots. Regardless of what part of the cannabis plant you proceed with, decarbing your raw plant matter is the most critical step in this process. Once you’ve made your decision on what material to steep your tea with, let’s talk about why properly debarbing your weed is so important, and how to brew yourself a cup of pure canna-bliss. What is decarboxylation and why does it matter? Decarboxylation is the process of activating the THC and CBD in your cannabis with heat so they can be properly absorbed within your body. When smoking or vaping cannabis, high heat from your combustion source causes this conversion process to happen instantly. When cannabis is used as an ingredient within edibles, this conversion must take place before their consumption for you to feel the psychoactive and medicinal effects of your weed. If you’re well versed in the world of edibles, the importance of the decarbing process comes as no surprise. If you’re new to this, don’t worry. We will break decarboxylation into easy-to-follow steps in this article on How to Properly Decarboxylate Your Cannabis. Following the steps and guidelines in this article closely during your decarbing process will be critical to the potency of your tea, and will give you the widest range of full-spectrum medicinal benefits from your cannabis. Cannabis Tea Recipes As with any other recipe, there are many different ways to achieve the final result of brewing weed tea. You can infuse your tea using cannabutter, coconut oil, infused sugars and tinctures, and more. Below we will explore a variety of methods for brewing cannabis tea, but it’s important to understand that cannabinoids must bind to fat molecules to be absorbed within your body, so using a source of fat helps to increase potency, absorption, and medicinal effects of your cannabis. Adding a fat source to tea might seem a bit out of the ordinary, but THC and CBD are lipophilic, which means they attract fat molecules. They are not water-soluble, so they do not fully dissolve in water alone. Scientific studies such as this one from 2016 show that the absorption of CBD and THC can increase by 2.5 – 3 times when adding a source of fat into the mix. Now that we’ve covered the science behind brewing potent cannabis tea, let’s start with the quickest, easiest-to-follow recipe for brewing this infused beverage. With just a few tools and ingredients, you’ll be brewing weed tea with the best of ’em in no time. Simple Herbal Weed Tea Recipe Tools and ingredients needed: 1 gram of cannabis material of your choice 1 tbsp of the fat source of your choice (We recommend unsalted butter or coconut oil) 1 teabag of your choice (Our favorites are citrus green tea and earl grey tea, but we recommend tea with strong flavor such as peppermint if you’d like to mask the potent weed flavor) 4 cups of water Sweetener of your choice (We like to use honey or agave nectar) A coffee filter, fine strainer, or cheesecloth A teapot or regular pot A spoon and mug Step 1: Decarboxylate your cannabis. Step 2: Finely grind your decarbed cannabis. Bring 4 cups of water to a boil on your stovetop or in an electric kettle. Carefully add 1 tablespoon of unsalted butter, coconut oil, or other fat source of choice, stirring vigorously until completely dissolved. Step 3: Slowly mix in your cannabis. Decrease the burner setting to low and let simmer for 10-15 minutes, stirring occasionally. Step 4: Put your strainer, coffee filter, or cheesecloth, over the opening of your mug. Pour your infused tea mixture into the mug through your strainer of choice to separate and discard the plant material. Step 5: Add a teabag of your choice to the mug, stirring in any sweeteners or spices you would like to add at this time. Cover and let your mixture steep for the recommended amount of time on your teabag (typically 5-15 minutes). Step 6: Kick back and relax with your mug of freshly brewed weed tea. Congratulations! You’re now a cannabis tea connoisseur. Pro Tip : If you have cannabutter, THC oil, tincture, or sugar on hand, these products are already infused and activated, and can simply be added after steeping a non-infused mug of tea to break this already easy recipe into even simpler steps: boil water, steep tea, stir in infused ingredient. Reusing Leftovers Additionally, the pulp leftover from making cannabis-infused butter and oils can be used to brew a delicious mug of weed tea. We’re all about reusing our materials, and this is an amazing way to squeeze every bit of goodness out of your cannabis. Simply place the cannabis pulp in a tea ball and steep alongside your teabag directly in your mug and enjoy! Infused Tea Potency and Consumption Cannabis te

THCV cannot break down into THC, it is two carbons shorter. The pentyl chain on THC is from its precursor, CBGA, which in turn gets it from it precursor, olivetolic acid. You'd have to add an ethyl to the end of THCV to make THC, and most drug metabolism in the body is taken care of by monooxygenases from the liver (cytochrome P450's) that just add -OH groups to something until it's water soluble enough to get flushed out by the kidneys. However, THCV and the other -varins get a propyl group instead of a pentyl group if there are shorter precursors present, with propyl groups coming off the aromatic ring. In the plant, these shorter precursors go through the same enzymes as their longer-chain counterparts would (like CBGA/ THCA/ CBDA/ CBCA synthases), and get folded in certain ways that leave the hydrocarbon chain exposed as a sort of tail. The cannabivarins interact with the receptors in a partially antagonistic way, because the pharmacophore of phytocannabinoids is that hydrocarbon chain coming off of that aromatic group. The lipophilic sidechain leading up to an aromatic ring with high electron density in the form of conjugated pi-orbitals closely mimics the electronic signature of the fatty acid amides such as anadamide (one of the endocannabinoid ligands for the CB1 receptor). So the longer than chain is, such as dimethylheptylpyran or tetrahydrocannabiphorol, the more agonism at the CB1 receptor you will observe, and inversely, the more truncated this sidechain length is, the more anatagonistic the receptor activity can get. In short, the more the cannabinoid resembles an omega-6 fatty acid, the more it resembles the endogenous ligand the receptor evolved to accept (which is why 5 carbons up to a double bonded 6th carbon is the magical spot for THC & CBD). Many of our receptors require a nitrogen somewhere in the structure for a drug or neurotransmitter to be active and that's what makes cannabinoids so unique in nature, they're not water soluble as well due to the lack of a free base (nitrogen's lone pair). You are stacking a lot of phytocannabinoids on top of each other so the interactions will be complex. I think your second assessment is accurate, that at a higher concentration THCV becomes a CB1R agonist, you have a lot of other CB1R agonists and allosteric modulators in that tincture of yours and that is the most abundant G-coupled protein receptor in the brain. There's plenty of them to bind to, and I would be willing to bet that 25 mg of THCV in a tincture by itself would not send you over the edge the same way that the entourage cocktail would. Perhaps titrate back to the 5-10 mg range for now.

Cannabis is very complex, so I don’t blame you. When you are choosing a CBD product (from industrial hemp), you have 3 options to choose from; Full Spectrum, Broad Spectrum, & Isolate. Full Spectrum is literally the entire hemp plant (CBD, CBG, CBN, CBC) but made specifically to have less than 0.3% delta-9 THC by dry weight. Broad Spectrum is just like Full, but just with undetectable levels of delta-9 THC. Isolate CBD is purified even more to just have CBD & nothing else. The gummies you have are full spectrum due to the delta-9 THC. Each gummy has 50mg of CBD & 5mg of THC, or in other words, a 10:1 CBD:THC ratio. Usually for beginners, it’s best to start with broad spectrum to test how you would react before jumping into THC, but it looks like you can handle it quite well. Regarding the drug test, you will most certainly test positive for delta-9 THC since you’re using full spectrum & even with broad spectrum; there’s no promises. I also advise against operating any vehicles while under the influence of cannabis since it alters your mood & thought process. Keep in mind that the more CBD there is, the less of the THC you’ll feel since CBD negates the effects of THC. Your CB1 & CB2 receptors in your brain are responsible on how you feel. Let me know if you have any other questions!

In general I prefer to be more grounded - introspective, curious, insightful, extroverted/loquacious, not so high/distractible that I can't safely drive if I need to, but high enough that I experience the world and universe a little bit differently than what's normal for me - but still well within the realm of what I imagine is normal for many other people. CBD is derived from Hemp, which contains a higher concentration of terpenes. Each terpene has its own set of benefits. Terpenes and all of the magic in hemp and cannabis plants are located inside trichomes, the hair-like structures found on the flowers, leaves, stems, and stalks of the hemp and cannabis plants. Trichomes cannot be seen with the naked eye, but can be viewed using a magnification app on a smart phone, jeweler's loupe, or inexpensive USB microscope at 40-45 percent magnification. Although there are hundreds of terpenes found in both Hemp and Cannabis plants, the most common ones found in CBD products are: Myrcene, Linalool, Limonene, Pinene, Caryophyllene, and Humulene. Each terpene has its own scent, effect, and health benefits. For example, Linalool is also present in lavender, long known for its pleasant and calming effects. In CBD, Linalool is good for insomnia, stress, anxiety, and pain. Limonene, with its citrus scents, is helpful for depression, anxiety, gastric reflux. My recommendation is to search online for a 'terpene wheel' which is a chart listing the most common terpenes and their benefits, then choose which terpenes are most likely to benefit the consumer. Note the relationship between terpenes and their scent and flavor. Limonene is associated with citrus scents and flavors, for example, so it is worthwhile to look at Citrus-flavored CBD products and read the labels to select one with a high concentration of Limonene. Full-Spectrum CBD products are often recommended, and they use the entire plant instead of only the leaves and flowers. Indeed, trichomes are also found on the stalks and stems. My second recommendation is to visit a CBD dispensary, where there staff is trained and more knowledgable about CBD than at a regular cannabis dispensary. (My apologies to bud tenders everywhere. Many bud tenders are very knowledgable, have professional training and education, and are dedicated to their craft especially in the CBD and medical marijuana space. However, this is not always the case in recreational dispensaries with newer bud tenders more focused on the recreational aspects and tend to recommend what is most popular or they personally are more familiar with.)

The 0.3% thc limit is on the dry weight measurement meaning that some full spectrum products can have lots of d9 thc in them. It’s best to go by the mg content and/or ratios. Less than 1mg per serving or 20:1 and up. Most full spectrum products are gonna have that and also be under 0.3% but since it’s legal to isolate thc from hemp and use it in cbd products you can end up with upwards of 5mg per serving. Lots of companies now have gummies that are around 5:1 and sometimes even 2:1. That’s a full on thc product right there. Yes it was extracted from hemp but delta 9 thc is the same molecule in each plant. So overall look for things that are under that 0.3% or under 1mg per serving. The best way to find that out is to look at the lab test and look for the total thc content. A 1000mg bottle is enough for around 20 servings so 3mg total means each serving has way less than 1mg so you’re good there. Most people take somewhere between 50-200mg of full spectrum extracts daily, best split into two servings morning and evening.

1. Bud. Aka flower, plant, loud, etc... Got your normal flower weed. You can roll it up with joint papers which give no flavor, or you can roll it up with a tobacco blunt wrap, which gives it favor and a little bit of a tobacco twist. You can also bowl smoke in pipes or a bong. You can even dry vaporize it, just as is in plant form. 2. Wax aka sauce, butter, shatter, diamonds, distillate (kinda), etc... This stuff normally looks like your average sticky wax, kind of resembles Candle wax what color and appearance can definitely change. With this stuff you typically grab a small glob, superheat (to a certain temp range) a glass bucket called a "banger or nail" (I use a terp slurper) that you put in your bong. Once the wax touches the hot glass it should vaporize on contact and you smoke it. There's also attachments that allow you to smoke it out of a pen or electronically without using a torch 3. Cartridges aka pens, dab pen, wax pen, cart, etc... Arguably your most common and concealable method of consumption, it is essentially wax in a syrupy viscosity which is then put into the cartridge with a small coil in it. Once you press the button or activate the battery that it's attached to it powers the coil, the coil heats and vaporizes the wax. 4. Edibles The most concealable but arguably the heaviest hitting. Edibles are foods that have THC mixed in with them, And usually take about 15 to 45 minutes to take effect. 100 mg is usually the amount you buy for one package of gummies or cookies. For a first time user 100 mg will knock you on your ass. Start slowly with 20 to 40 and experiment with how much gets you to where you need to be, be extremely careful with edibles because they tend to get people really bad experiences and can be overpowering. I tried to explain this to a friend the other day before he bought his pax. Pax is kind of like the apple brand of the vape world. It does what it does well and is fairly reliable but overall the technology used is really cheap and found on $50 vapes and it rarely changes. Similar to an iPhone. I use an iPhone but realistically my iPhone 11s is no different than my buddies iPhone 14. Just like my iPhone 11 was almost no different from my 8. They just don’t change or really do anything new with the technology like other brands. They just release them because people buy them. Pax is like that. They became a big name and are sold everywhere. People unknowingly claim they are the best. Realistically they use full conduction which is what all the cheap vapes use. It just comes in a nice sleek package and looks good. It’s not worth $200. Not to say conduction is awful because it has its place but realistically it’s a cheaper technology that’s done even better with a butane vape. The mighty is a far better product using hybrid heating but relies a lot on convection. Something like the tinymight 2 uses full convection and can heat up on 3-5s allowing on demand hits. So to me that kind of technology is worth $350. A pax is basically just an overpriced vape that you could get similar quality out of for under $75. For $75 you can start getting hybrid heating that works a lot better. If you like big milky clouds with subpar taste then the pax is your go to. If you like big clouds and decent flavor that you can do a whole session on then the mighty+ is what you want. If you want full convection that doesn’t waste flower, heats up instantly, taste amazing, all while hitting hard you want something like a Tinymight 2 or Roffu if you’re on a budget.

My experience show us that everyone differs however we know from a decade of feedback that CBG seems to be a popular solution to number one on your list Sativa dominant products whether they are isolated , broad spectrum or full spectrum might be a good place to start with regards to number two on your list 😁 we think it’s mainly about the terpene set when it comes to uplifting the mood / energizing the mind. There are products on the market which include active ingredients aimed at sleep - which may or may not induce or help sleep however, we just advise taking CBD oil regularly at medium dose for two weeks to see if it helps you sleep … lots of customers feedback indicates full spectrum or broad spectrum with some minor cannabinoids can be more effective, but in the UK, these products are limited… and if Cbd is going to help you sleep then you will probably be able to tell after the regular use of any high-quality CBD product. If the product is labeled or advertised as broad spectrum you would expect to see a measurable amount of CBG or minor cannabinoid within the formulation… not just a milligram or two! So check the lab results before you buy. Loads of products are called these names but really they are just cbd isolate which all has a trace of CBDv - it’s not broad spectrum, it’s pulling the wool over your eyes. A truly broad spectrum product is either derived from its original broad spectrum extract or formulated to be truly broad spectrum product If you’re not sure, the best place to start is probably a broad spectrum product … one which contains CBG, which you can actually read the level on the product certificate of analysis 🧐 really everyone differs I hope you find what you’re looking for 😀 Cbd - equals cbd only products which don’t contain more than trace cbd variants (cbdv, cbdA, etc). Most isolates used today all contain these traces - this does not make the end product broad spectrum. Broad spectrum cbd = cbd which also contains other cannabinoids above 0.2% in my book anyway! And has terpenes which are either added to the product or present in the original hemp extract. Full spectrum = as above with trace THC at a legal limit - you may find full spectrum products also contain more crude oil so flavonoids and other waxes not commonly found in refined extracts or isolates. My experience : 12 years in the field 😉 But we all have our own opinions I believe because we all react differently to Cannabinoid ratios and products - it’s natural due to the individual makeup of our EndoCannabinoid systems. You will do doubt need to try and few. Feel free to ask for recommendations!

I smoked a lot of hash in the US in the early 2000s. Some of it was really good, but you either had to pay a lot for it ($25-40/g) or know a grower, and it was mainly used with flower in a joint or bowl, or in edibles. It was rarely used by itself (though hot knives were population) without flower, so it was seen as a novelty additive more than anything. Then butane hash oil started getting big around 05, and we switched to other concentrates. Hash is still a thing though, particularly bubble hash. Its mostly made and smoked by older generations. A dispensary near me sometimes carries it, but more often it's pressed into rosin (which is very expensive). Dry sift is pretty popular too, which is essentially unpressed hash. It is a different high, though, as the pressing and oxidizing of real hash mellows out the high in a relaxing way. So most people that want hash can make it at home. The black market is full of cheap, potent bud and concentrates, so hash just don't really fit in, demand wise.

Anything they sell you for $1 is usually a last chance for them to get rid of product that is expiring or over a specific manufacturing date. One good practice is to ask your budtender to see the product in package, and check the label for a Manufacturing or Best By date. That should give you some idea of how old the oil is. That isn’t always definitive though. You could have some companies holding on to flower/distillate for months to years before they process and test. California Dept of Public Health requires best by dates to be 1 year after the test date. I’m not sure what PA’s requirements are. And “expired” to one person vs another could be up for debate. We have OG weed heads in their 50s that say “weed never expires” lol, but the composition definitely changes like any other product.

Add olive oil to the infused coconut oil. (This helps create a smoother finished product and improves absorption.) Mix in or whisk the beeswax until it is melted. (The beeswax reduces greasiness.) I don't know which ones you had, but half a bag would be either 250 mg of Delta 8 THC, or 250 mg of Delta 9 THC or 250 mg of something called Off-Spec. So you have no good reason to be scared of CBD products.

It's wonderful to hear that you've had positive experiences with edibles and that you're noticing positive changes in your mood and overall well-being. Cannabis, including its various compounds like THC and CBD, can have a range of effects on individuals, and it's not uncommon for people to report improved mood, relaxation, and increased pleasure. The shift in your perspective and emotional state is indeed something that others have experienced. Cannabis can influence neurotransmitters and receptors in the brain, potentially leading to changes in mood, perception, and emotional experiences. However, it's essential to approach cannabis use with a mindful and informed perspective. While many people find positive effects, individual responses can vary. It's crucial to be aware of potential side effects, and in some cases, excessive use may lead to negative experiences. As for the sustainability of these positive changes, it's challenging to predict. Some individuals find that the positive effects are consistent, while others may experience fluctuations. Factors such as dosage, frequency of use, and individual differences play a role. If you're concerned about slipping back into old patterns or if you have questions about the impact of cannabis on your mental well-being, it's advisable to discuss your experiences with a healthcare professional. They can provide personalized guidance based on your health history, lifestyle, and goals. Feeling isolated or uncertain about your experiences is entirely understandable, and reaching out for support or having open conversations with loved ones can be beneficial. Each person's journey with cannabis is unique, and your feelings and reflections are valid.

When you’re taking any cannabinoid orally you need to pair it with fat. So at the very least you should be combining the isolate with MCT oil or another carrier. But even then, if I take 100mg of CBD oil (whether isolate or full spectrum) on an empty stomach, I get much less effect than when I take it with a high fat meal (like the Chinese food I had last night). So the fat in the carrier oil helps in metabolizing it, and the more additional fat helps even more. I see you’re not someone who uses THC, like myself, but there’s a reason that people eating pot brownies became a thing. In addition to the heating process itself, called decarboxylation, it’s the high fat content of brownies that have made them a cliche when talking about edibles. Cannabinoids are fat soluble. When your intestines absorb the fat you get to experience the cannabinoids. Try 100mg with the fattiest meal you can stand to see the difference lol. Additionally, there are water soluble versions of CBD and THC made using a “nano encapsulation process” (whatever that means) and you just put that in a drink, no need for fat. I got a low priced powdered version of this from Gilded Extracts that I use daily, in addition to taking the more common fat soluble oil with my breakfast. The water soluble version works in like 20min and has a more pronounced and obvious effect. And since you mention sleep, the best choice for that is CBN (also needs the fat and best when combined with CBD and a little THC). But what you have WILL work for you. This is simply not true. The main difference in the subjective feeling between smoking and eating THC is the ratio of THC to the more potent 11-hydroxy-THC. The conversion process mostly happens in the liver, but smoking bypasses the liver and lets THC directly into the bloodstream, with only a small amount of conversion happening in the lungs. Below is a relevant quote from an article I found and the article is also linked. But my main point is that no one would actually ever lack this enzyme because it is crucial to the metabolism of so many compounds and would cause a systemic crisis if the body was unable to break down and eliminate these drugs from the body. This means that for those who don't feel much effect from eating cannabis, especially if they do get high from smoking, it is more likely that the enzymes in their liver are breaking down both THC and 11-hydroxy-THC too quickly for them to take effect. “The real difference between edibles and smoking or vaping is that with edibles, a much larger fraction of Delta-9-THC makes it to the liver first. There it gets converted to 11-hydroxy-THC,” Jikomes says. “So in other words, if you smoke or vape, the ratio of 11-hydroxy-THC to Delta-9-THC is quite low, and if you take an edible it's much higher.”
