Oh wow no caffeine that's very cool. 👍♥️ The best way to use CBD is: 1. Take it daily, and try to take the same amount every day. Consistency makes it work better. You won't ruin anything though if something happens on a day that causes you to end up needing some more after dealing with that thing (like say this "something" ruined your day and your earlier dose is definitely not cutting it, even a couple of hours after the "something" thing is over with). Just don't make a habit of it because then you won't be taking it consistently. 2. To find out how much you need to take, start with a low dose and slowly increase until you find your optimal dose (I guess give each new level a few days to see how it goes so that you get a miniature consistent dosing experience with the new level). You can keep going beyond that dose if you want though just to see if perhaps you still need more. When you reach a point where you're good and you don't need any more help from CBD, you can keep that as your daily dose. There are people who are finding very noticeable benefits from just a few milligrams and find very noticeable differences when they adjust their dose by a milligram or two, but then of course the range goes all the way to the people who need a few hundred milligrams in each dose, some have to do that multiple times per day! The only way to know how much you need is by experimenting. You're basically looking for your daily maintenance dose which is where you're not taking more than you need. 3. When you take some, wait 15 to 120 minutes to judge the effects of how much you took and remember that how this dose is affecting you the first time might be very different to how it's affecting you in a few days. Sometimes you can experience an illusion it's not working as well as the first time since now you're more used to it. 4. Always shake the bottle well before putting any into the dropper. 5. Don't touch the dropper with your tongue, but find a way to get your dose under your tongue (I use a spoon) and leave it there for a minimum of about 30 seconds, but ideally longer like for a few minutes. I like to do 5 minutes since it's a nice round number. This helps get the beneficial compounds that are floating around in the MCT carrier oil into your bloodstream faster. So you don't have to put it under your tongue. Not putting it under your tongue slightly lengthens the amount of time it takes to begin feeling the benefits. I think I'm all out of tips. So, 5 tips it is! I like that. It's a nice round number. Generally you will always get better results from something that has all the cannabinoids and terpenes in rather than a distillate or isolate. I assumed there would of been a detectable amount of THC in your buds TBH as I've never once had any that have actually had 0%/non detectable, just levels below 0.2% are allowed in the UK, and the limit I think is higher in USA (0.3% maybe?), and IIRC they also allow high THCa which will convert to THC when decarbed/heated. Personally I make my carts/oil with distillates, broad spectrum distillates, and sometimes added isolates, but the final mixture will have more cannabinoids in much higher percentages than even flower, so it's basically all the components dissected and put back together. It's hard for me to explain or give an example with CBD as it's not psychoactive, but if for example you make a cart with just THC distillate in it, it will get you high and be strong, but if you add loads of CBD and other cannabinoids, as well as terpenes etc.. even though the final mix has less THC than before, like half of what it would be with straight distillate, the high is better, more rounded, and lasts a lot longer. It's a strange thing, because if you did a blind test you would assume the mixed cart has more THC due to the better and longer lasting high. Entourage effect basically, but it's very hard to notice when THC is not in the mix, at least for me anyway. Personally I don't find CBD very useful at all on it's own, or even mixed in a broad spectrum oil/cart. CBN I always have a good 3-5% in my mixes and notice that a lot, it makes me nice and sleepy, but again that is easy to notice because it is psychoactive unlike the majority of the other cannabinoids.

Hey friend, Your Doc’s (at least I’d hope) are going to be giving you advice based on their clinical judgement. With Crohn’s, you gotta keep in mind that your body is in an already immunocompromised state (or at least during an active flare). Smoking anything is going to lead to even further inflammation, and can probably trigger further inflammatory responses in your bowels. Further, just as a common rule, any psychoactive drug use with a mental disorder carries risk of psychosis or psychotic breaks. Some people can use drugs (not just trees) while dealing with a variety of mental ailments and never have anything happen, and someone with undiagnosed asymptomatic schizophrenia can be thrown into a years long bout of psychosis, and at the end of the day we don’t know enough in the mental health world to really give you the differentiating factor between those two patients. First things first, take care of your health, and then from there maybe look for alternatives to smoking (i.e., edibles, tinctures, etc). Health is #1, your cannabis use needs to be #2. Take care of yo’ self. 🙏🏻

Any half reputable CBD company will have COAs available for their products. If you scroll down on the page you linked, there’s this link under “Product Documentation”: On the COA, there are LOD and LOQ values. The LOD is the minimum amount of a chemical required to be present, in order for the lab to confidently say it is present. LOQ is the minimum amount required to be present for the lab to confidently be able to analyze concentration taking into consideration testing imprecision and accuracy bias. If a chemical is ND it means it is below the LOD. It is impossible to accurately say how much THC is present, but you should be able to be confident it is below the listed LOD. So in the example of the most recent COA they have posted, the LOD of THCa is 0.062mg/g. This means the tincture has less than 0.062mg/g.

Yeah I'd say you did. Edibles in general are a fucking scam these days. I was getting the gummy's for a while because people like them but they're nowhere near the potency they claim and the effect is very one dimensional because they use distillate which is just basically pure THC. I'm old-school, I got into the game making brownies back in the day and I recently returned to my roots. It took a little nudging to catch on because everybody's brother made a shitty batch of brownies that didn't do shit once so people don't trust homemade edibles but I make mine right. I make my own extracts at home with a magic budder maker, which means full spectrum extracts. I'm not just spraying on some THC distillate or melting in some crappy wax that I couldn't sell, you are getting everything that could possibly survive the process, all of the cannabinoids and addition amount of turpines as well. Turpines won't survive the baking or cooking process so that's not as important, but you could also make a tincture and take that directly where those turpines may come into play for their different medical uses. The effect is noticeably different, it is more of a psychedelic experience in a way, the body high is more euphoric and you get the giggles like we did back in the day when we first started smoking. If you have access to Bud I would highly recommend looking into making homemade extracts. You don't have to use a machine like mine and you don't have to use butter or even oil, which are considerably more touchy than my preferred method, the Everclear method. A lot of the failed brownie batches back in the day were because somebody burned the butter or didn't simmer the oil long enough or fucked up straining it and lost a lot of product. With the Everclear method there is no cooking required unless you have my machine. You simply chop up and decarboxylate your bud and then you add Everclear to it and put it in a cool dark place for a week or two. Give it a good shake every once in a while And when it seems saturated strain out the plant matter. The only difference in what I do is the machine heats and agitates it over an eight hour period to speed the process up. You can allow excess alcohol to evaporate or add more to your desired strength, after that you can add it to pretty much any baked good or alcoholic drink. There is another method though, where I got my start all those years ago. Take your bud, decarboxylate, grind that shit into a powder and just throw it in the mix. The texture might be a little bit off and some people might complain but you'll be lit. The most important part is proper decarboxylation, I do 240° for 45 minutes. My one warning for this method is if you use really high-grade bud for this there is a chance you might black out. I know that sounds ridiculous but It happened to multiple people multiple times

Most people think when they see 84% thc that it’s the same as thc percentage that you see on flower . What it actually means is that the other 16% is the butane left in the oil (or distillate )when it’s extracted . Also the terpenes added to the oil to give you the different strains / different highs have vitamin e acetate along with other additives that are not good for you . So a cheap unrefined oil mixed with terpenes that have all kinds of additives is how you get cheap carts . Spend a little more on live rosin carts or full spectrum carts without added terpenes , or make sure you’re getting a distillate cart with a higher thc content

Hi there. I'm in cannabis medicine, have ADHD and a daily smoker. Cannabis can have different effects in different people based in genetics, your endocannabinoid system and tolerance. The indica Vs Sativa effect is a myth and effects of a strain are related to the chemical profile: the phytocannabioids(thc/cbd/cbg...) alongside terpenes (essential oils of a plant) and flavinoids( what gives it its color, flavor and scent as well). Certain strains of cannabis can help with ADHD as others mentioned, it helps quiet the "chatter " and thus help you focus. It basically makes you more mindful of ur current status and thus able to control impulses-maning able to focus. It can also can be inhibitive to your motivation as well. People with ADHD are at higher risk for addictions because of lack of impulse control and chasing immediate gratification. Basically be aware of your dose and research strains that can be helpful. What works for someone also might not work well for you. Strains that contain the terpene Pinene can help with focus and ADHD. Good luck and do your research too. Leafly is a great website and App to use as a resource.

Use as much water as you want. THC is waterproof otherwise it would wash way in the rain. Helps to splash some extra water on the mulch and give it a good squeeze to get all the remaining THC out. However squeezing every last drop of THC out of the mulch and also grinding it down really fine to get all the THC you can, will give you alot more of that nasty plant matter. I like to make a very refined MCT coconut oil for candy making and drinks where I use top quality flower and dont press down on the mesh or grind it up too fine. Just kind of gravity filter it through a cheesecloth it for a few seconds. Then toss the remaining oil soaked mulch into a cannabutter batch filled with trim and larf, you can even toss in moldy or mildewy weed if it isn't too bad lol. Grind it to dust, squeeze the life out of the mulch, then filter the butter out with water a bunch of times You can use a paper towel if you dont have a cheesecloth but be careful not to cook it on high heat as high heat will degrade the THC.

The vendor I use has 30 gram jars of 95% D8 distillate, the remaining percentage is CBN and other cannabinoids Distillate is a little more tricky to handle than Isolate because it’s so sticky, and there are a couple extra steps involved in making a tincture from Distillates. The differences are - - You have to keep the distillate at room temp, so it’s not too sticky/gooey, or too hard from being too cold or else it’ll shatter all over the place. Use something hard like 1 scissor blade to get it out of the container. Use a toothpick or another pair is scissors to get it off the original scissors. - Cut a 2x2 inch square of wax paper, and put that on your scale. The distillate comes off of that perfectly. Make sure to TARE the wax paper first lol - After you weigh the distillate and it’s on the wax paper, stick it in the freezer for 2 minutes so it hardens up. - Use your fingers to pull it off and plop em into the em into the empty tincture bottle - Pour in the MCT oil - Give it a hot water bath, to melt the Distillate into the oil, by Boiling some water, and pour the pre-boiled water into a regular ceramic Coffee cup ☕️ - Put the almost finished tincture bottle into the boiled water cup, let it sit a couple minutes, pull it out and shake it up really hard, put it back in there, repeat u til you don’t see any distillate chunks floating around. If it’s not melted yet by the time the water isn’t that hot, just pour more boiled water into the cup so it’s hot again. After a couple times, it’ll be fully dissolved and ready to use. Sometimes I have to re-fill the cup with boiled water if I put large Distillate chunks in there. That’s it 👍

While I do agree with your sentiment, I think THC and CBD helped extend my dogs life by another year. We had a german shorthaired pointer who was coming close to 13, and had all sorts of health problems. She had a tumor on her leg and pretty bad breathing problems. We brought her into the vet to consider options, but she was right around her life expectancy and probably wouldn't have been able to extend her life much longer anyways. A few weeks later, her breathing got really bad again. It wouldn't slow down and we thought she was going to die that night. I was kind of panicking but wanted to do something for her, so I gave her some thc/cbd peanut butter I had. An hour later, and her breathing calmed down completely. It was the calmest we had seen her in awhile, and she was seemingly enjoying the high. I knew dogs weren't supposed to have any, so I was expecting her to maybe get a little sick from it or go into a panic, but she just sat there just as I would - super calm and red-eyed with a shitfaced grin. For the next year, I kept feeding her THC/CBD peanut butter sandwiches and it seemed to do wonders for her. I think she started to recognize it too, because she would start to give me signs she wants some when her breathing would get bad. She started hounding me anytime I would start rolling. It got to a point where even my buddies were telling me my dog was a fiend. She ended up living another year after the really bad night, at which point things got bad enough we had to put her down. And while I definitely agree getting pets high isn't cool, it was nice to see my dog was somewhat of an outlier and the peace and comfort it brought her in her final year. RIP Jackie, you were a real one </3

Article text: >Medical cannabis became Dr. Lynn Parodneck’s professional focus in 2016, not long after seeing how the plant helped treat her husband’s Parkinson’s Disease symptoms. >But in 2022, Parodneck, a Bedford-based doctor who previously ran a private obstetrics and gynecology practice in Manhattan, noticed some of her cannabis patients dropping off when it was time to recertify with her. A few told her they received a mailer from Leafwell offering recertification telehealth appointments for $99. >”Once a month they blast these things – “renew cheap and easy” – which is pretty disgusting,” Parodneck said. >Cannabis telemedicine company Leafwell, in partnership with medical cannabis company Curaleaf – and possibly other medical operators in New York – have been emailing medical cannabis patients who have subscribed to mailing lists with offers of lower-cost telehealth certifications, both companies acknowledged to NY Cannabis Insider. Both companies said they only send these mailers to patients who actively opt in to their list. >However, the partnership between the two companies appears to violate medical cannabis regulations. >According to rules for medical cannabis businesses, “No marketing or advertising of medical cannabis products shall:... cooperate, directly or indirectly, in any advertising if such advertising has the purpose or effect of steering or influencing patient or caregiver choice with regard to the selection of a practitioner.” >When NY Cannabis Insider asked the Office of Cannabis Management if the mailers advertising discounted certifications violate regulations, a spokesperson didn’t say whether this specific activity breaks the rules. >”Any registered organization found to have violated medical regulations will be subject to applicable penalties,” OCM spokesperson Aaron Ghitelman said in an email response. “As with all reports of potential misconduct, the Office of Cannabis Management reviews any and all allegations and if warranted, conducts an investigation and makes a determination based on Cannabis Law and regulations.” >Parodneck and others also say that the certification method is detrimental to patients, in addition to diminishing their medical practices. >Leafwell’s telehealth model incentivizes quick consultations with patients who may not get all the information they need about issues like prescription drug interactions or which marijuana products are better for different situations and ailments. >Meanwhile, a patient advocate says that while telehealth poaching can harm some patients, it can be a lifeline for others. >”I think there’s plenty of us who are able to self-manage and can educate ourselves enough, but there’s definitely some patients who do need a little bit more of an active hand in their care,” said Tim Mitchell, a medical cannabis patient and advocate. “It’s those more vulnerable patients who would be the ones to suffer.” >New York State’s medical cannabis program requires patients to get certified by a registered medical practitioner who may prescribe marijuana for various qualifying conditions. Patients must recertify at least once per year to remain on the medical program. >Parodneck approaches her medical cannabis practice in a community-focused manner, she said. My buddy stayed over at my house and he brought some gummies that his work friend had made and given to him. I just kept going up, and up, and up, and up, higher and higher with no end in sight. I remember looking at my buddy’s eyes and internally panicking because his eyes were so red I was convinced he was bleeding out of his eyes. I had genuinely never seen that shade of red in someone’s eyes before but I still had a tiny bit of logic left in my monkey brain to remember that weed does that. We were staying in my parents’ camper in my backyard and at this point my parents didn’t know I partook. Around midnight, an outside light that can only be turned on from inside the garage came on and we immediately freaked out because we thought my mom was coming outside to check on us and we were totally blasted off of this planet. We scrambled in our cannabis induced stupor to shut off all the lights in the camper and make it look like we were asleep and as I lay in bed for what felt like hours waiting for my mom to come into the camper (she never did) I sent myself into a panic thinking about how much trouble I would be in if she knew and how disappointed she would be in me because she hated weed at the time due to my dead beat druggie uncles. My heart rate was beating faster than a formula 1 car’s engines and I couldn’t breathe and my stomach was twisting in knots. At some point I convinced myself I was having a heart attack and was about to die and I was begging my friend to call an ambulance but he just kept reassuring me that I was fine and told me to shut up and go to bed. He said if I still felt like I was dying in the morning he would take me to the hospital and that sounded reasonable enough to my high ass so I just went to bed and I was fine in the morning lol.

They can’t read the article, because what you posted is not an article. It is a summary of an article. It has no methodology section, no results/findings section, no appendices describing measures. They do not appear to adequately account for confounding variables. They are making medical claims from self-reported data. They do not have proper temporal ordering to make causal claims, and the researchers know this. That’s why they say “association” and “linked” instead of “causally related”. This is they type of research your research methods/stats professor would call “junk in - junk out”. Anybody can get a computer to take hundreds of thousands of data points and get it to spit out statistically significant correlations. That does not mean the results are particularly noteworthy or valid. But if the analysis is competently done, well written, and you send it to enough journals you can eventually get half-ass research projects published. Blind peer-review at research journals isn’t really blind. Don’t dismiss, but be intellectually critical. Bit fkn messy but you need, One large box of nerds - the rainbow ones are my fave Whatever haribo gummies you like the taste of (haribo melts the best) - melt your gummies in a double boiler on low, you can add a table spoon of water to help them reach a nice consistency. You want it to be smooth and when you lift the spoon from the bowl and drizzle the mixture back in, it should slightly hold its shape on the surface before sinking into the mix within 5-8 seconds - let your gummy mix sit for a few minutes (sometimes takes up to 10 to cool enough) before dumping the whole large pack of nerds in that bad boy. Mix it together with the spoon and pop down some baking paper onto your working bench. - to roll the gummy nerds mix without it sticking to everything you own, wet down your hands and shake off excess water for like 1 second so it’s not dripping, pick up teaspoon size amounts and roll them in your palm until they form a rough ball. Put them onto the baking paper and let them cool for 1-2hrs before eating That recipe makes a great amount for under $10 and they’re nerds all the way through encased in gummy. I’d say roughly 1.5g-4.5g per tincture, per cannabinods, depending on how you want it to feel. To make a tincture, get avocado or MCT oil, some sunflower or soy lecithin, and a candle or oven. You can heat the disty in the oven at 180f until it’s liquidy, or what I’ve found is easier, is to just heat it over a small candle flame, making sure to not let the heat sit on one spot so you don’t burn it and distribute the heat evenly. Then pour it into some glass dropper bottles, roughly 6g/6mL(of d8) if you’re using a syringe, add 6g of lecithin, and then fill it up most of the way with your carrier oil. Then heat this over a candle flame, in front of a heater, or in the oven, and shake it around to ensure the D8 mixes(heating is to make it dissolve quicker) .

I think we are kind-of venturing into pedantry here - but the topic interests me: [ "Categorizing cannabis as either “Sativa” and “Indica” has become an exercise in futility. Ubiquitous interbreeding and hybridization renders their distinction meaningless. The arbitrariness of these designations is illustrated by “AK-47,” a hybrid that won “Best Sativa” in the 1999 Cannabis Cup, and won “Best Indica” four years later.[^(73)]( More than 30 years ago, unhybridized plants of Indian heritage and Afghani landraces were already difficult to obtain.[^(62)]( Hybridization has largely obliterated population differences, “especially between the two kinds of fiber forms and between the two kinds of marijuana forms." and "In today's vernacular taxonomy, “Ruderalis” is applied to plants that exhibit one to three characteristics: CBD≅THC, wild-type morphology, or early flowering (sometimes called “autoflowering,” that is, day-neutral, flowering not induced by light cycle). Some authors have tried to reconcile “Sativa” and “Indica” with formal C. sativa and C. indica . McPartland et al.[^(65)]( noted that Afghani plants were mislabeled “Indica.” They reassigned “Indica” to Vavilov's taxon, at species rank ( Cannabis afghanica ) or varietal rank ( C. sativa var. afghanica ). In summary, reconciling the vernacular and formal nomenclatures: “Sativa” is really indica , “Indica” is actually afghanica , and “Ruderalis” is usually sativa . All three are varieties of one species, C. sativa L." From my reading, and feel free to correct me, when we talk about sativa vs indica vs ruderalis, we are engaging in "vernacular taxonomy," and essentially we are just talking about different varietals, like discussing the differences in types of corn or tomatoes- some of its got lots of sugars, some of it is bred for animal feed, some of it is bred for biomass for fuel, some of it grows big fruits and some small, some fruit until frost, others grow a set amount and stop, but they are all the same plant. Same thing with cannabis - especially today with all the work that has been done to cross breed high thc genetics into autoflowering strains - and I dont know if we can anylonger say "ruderalis produces very small buds with very little cannabinoids." They're expensive but pay themselves off quickly, and your lungs will thank you. My wife and I bought a volcano using money my parents very graciously gave us for Christmas (they'd be so proud). Also the residue left behind in the bag is called "vapor honey" and still has a thc content. If you really want you can cut the bag open and scrape it off when you switch to a new bag. I've done it but don't really think it's worth it. But it's another way to get more out of your bud. When cleaning your grinder, use 90+% isopropyl alcohol (the higher the % the better). Don't pour the alcohol down the drain, pour it through a strainer onto a glass plate and let it air dry completely. The residue left behind is basically hash and can be scraped off with a razor blade. Keep stems from your bud to add into the alcohol to get more hash. If you have an mflb, punch a hole through one of the battery caps, stick the stem through it, and use that as an adapter to hit your mflb through a bong. I’ve never thought about it really, but I think I actually do? 😅 It’s not at all exciting, but it is real. This usually takes 2-4 hours and I would say this happens 90% of the time. I sit on the bathroom floor with the fan on and a candle lit. I use a bong with a shit ton of ice. Listen to my favorite music while it kicks in. If you want a feel for the vibe, listen to Be Sweet by Japanese Breakfast. I get up shortly after before the full effects take hold. Then I actually take a few minutes to clean up and leave no trace. For some reason that part is satisfying to me - like it’s a secret that I only want to know. I grab my headphones that I leave in a kitchen drawer and go to my back porch. I sit there for at least 10-20 minutes just listening to my favorites. I know that it’s kicked in because my mind starts to pick apart lyrics and relate it deeply to my life. That’s when it’s time to go inside. I grab my favorite cup and fill it to the brim with ice and water. I grab my favorite snack - salt and vinegar almonds. I go to my bedroom. I lay down with my headphones still in. Windows open so the sun shines on my face. Now it’s fully kicked in and I’m having really hazy deep thoughts. Sometimes I stop and write them down into my notes app. Most times, I turn on my favorite show right now, don’t laugh, Gilmore Girls. Something about them living a normal life feels comforting to me. I watch for an episode or two until the horniness kicks in. It always kicks in and it’s always amazing. Headphones still in too. Afterwards, I chill for a little with my music back on, but this time no headphones. I do that for a while until I finally get up. I make a bath. Eucalyptus mint bubble bath and the lights off. Sometimes a candle. I just lay in the water with Gilmore Girls still on in the background. I just float for an hour or two with my thoughts and my sour grape vape. It’s my bathtub vape lol. Dry myself off and get back into bed. I don’t get dressed because one of my favorite feelings is cold clean skin on a toasty bed. I just cuddle up with my snack and watch a list of content that I really love. There’s a peak into my life! And what I will be doing tonight lol

I worked as a deliver for a cannabis bakery that included fizzy THC drinks. I had access to free samples of everything in the store.. cookies, rice krispy treats, lollipops, butter.. the drinks were definitely the strongest for me, and I really didn’t enjoy it. I had a few extreme overdoses where I was rolling on the ground in my backyard when I ate the whole cookie or the entire krispy treat (don’t ever eat the whole cookie). It’s a few years later and I only consume edibles in the form of gummies - and I try to eat only enough to experience a slight impact whether it is a sativa uplifting one or an indica relaxing one. My problem with the drinks was all about portion tracking. How much THC is in a gulp? What if I drink a half bottle of the drink.. nope that was too much. It was just so dang strong and hard to find the right amount that gave me a feeling that didn’t cause a giant headache afterwards.. every single drink (even just a gulp) would hit me with such a tremendous high and the amplitude would skyrocket so fast, such that I just didn’t enjoy it and couldn’t tell where the buzz finally plateaued .. it would just go up and up and up, and then I just felt like I would need a 14 hour nap. I spent a few years truly enjoying smoking joints with not too much THC. I had one year with a successful garden of Durban Poison (the most extreme sativa strain I have ever encountered) and I actually loved smoking it, but I found if I only had a few deep puffs that I could truly enjoy it and still be productive in daily activities (I work from home as a devops / release engineer and also get in the garden every day). I love that I can regulate my intake with accuracy down to a ‘half puff’ - that level of atomic accuracy allows me to know how much I am consuming without taking it too far. It really sucks to be rolling on the ground for hours waiting for THC to pass out of your system.

Hey man, it's definitely possible to be allergic to weed. Some people can have reactions like itchy eyes, a heavy feeling, or even feeling foggy and drowsy. Those symptoms can be related to the weed itself or even to how it's grown, like if there are molds or pesticides present. The fact that you don't experience this with hash is interesting. Hash is made differently, so maybe the process eliminates something in weed that you're sensitive to. Could also be the specific strains you're smoking. Terpenes, the compounds in weed that give it its smell, can sometimes cause allergic reactions too. It's a bit of a guessing game without more info, but maybe try different strains or even edibles to see if you react differently. Have you ever tried talking to others who have similar reactions to see what worked for them?

Not a dumb question at all! When it says to place the tincture under your tongue, you’re actually aiming for what's called sublingual absorption. Basically, you're supposed to let the liquid sit under your tongue so that the blood vessels there can absorb the THC. You don’t need to keep your tongue lifted; just let the tincture chill under there for about 30 seconds to a minute, then swallow. That way, the THC hits your bloodstream faster than if you just swallowed it straight away. If your tongue happens to touch the tincture, it's no biggie, but the absorption happens more efficiently under your tongue, not through the tongue itself. The goal is to get it absorbed quickly, so try to resist the urge to swish it around or swallow too soon.

What you and the previous comment that seems to have been deleted now are basing your info on is outdated, or will be it seems.. From the 2024 farm bill.. The amendment specifies that hemp would exclude non-naturally occurring synthetic and intoxicating products (i.e., allows only naturally occurring or derived nonintoxicating products). While the amendment does not define intoxicating, it would prohibit hemp cannabinoid products with “quantifiable amounts” of Total THC (including THCA) or any other cannabinoids that have (or are marketed to have) “similar effects on humans or animals” as THC, as determined by USDA. This would not prohibit all hemp cannabinoid products, such as CBD, but would require determinations by USDA based on available scientific research and quantification methods.

You get it from Cannabis Sativa L. with less than .3% THCD9. Defined decades prior to legalization in 2014 and the 2018 farm bill, see 7 U.S.C § 1639o. DEA carved out an exception for CBD under 31, tetrahydrocannabinol. not marijuana 23, or marijuana extracts 58, since public safety concern had to do with the psycho activity caused by the chemical compound, THC, not the plant. See 21 CFR § 1308.11 (d)(31)(ii). Naturally, any extraction of THC (d8, d9, or otherwise), from the hemp plant would be covered under other laws, including 31 or more importantly 58, which has very little definition to it. BUT the 2001 exemption, § 1308.35, Combined with the 31 Carve out mentioned above, would make any such extraction, including natural processing like Keif, from any type of cannabis sativa L. plant product designed for human consumption or a product that’s controlled by the FDA or other, aka animal feed, vape cartridges, topical, joints and brownies. All your products are only legal in your state alone only if your state has language in their laws for it. And no “we legalized weed” doesn’t count.

It wasnt a huge piece, like a regular Hersheys bar amount, plus my self control is lacking sometimes.. I put the wrapper in my backpack and went to the bus stop. 40 mins later Im on the metro bus in downtown Seattle and I started to feel the almost narcotic-like feeling of a HEAVY dose of edibles, Im no newbie.. eating over 1,000 mgs of thc in edibles wasnt a rarity for me. Not trying to flex and frankly i dont think im alone.. lol! Anyways, What im getting at is Im very familiar with the way a little bit of edibles feels when its kicking in, versus a LOT of edibles.. I remembered the wrapper in my bag, pulled it out and saw that it was 40mgs per serving".. and there was 10 servings. Lol.. ... ... 400mgs on an empty stomach with zero tolerance. My mind was racing "what does a normal person who is not high on drugs do.. how do they do.." so I decided on just looking out the window. After this point I kinda took a step back, challenged my perception of reality and realized Im just baked as fuck. Start low and slow. You can always eat another gummy or smoke more, but you can’take your body process it any faster For smoking joints/blunts: try to draw the smoke into your mouth first before inhaling. This helps cool the smoke a bit so you won’t cough as much. Take a drag or two and pass to the next person. If you don’t feel anything by the time it gets back to you, repeat until you’re comfortable. Remember it’s like alcohol: once you stop, you’ll continue to feel the effects for awhile afterwards. And it’ll take a bit after you stop for it to peak in your system. So stop just short of “hoo boy I’m stoned” to avoid getting nauseous. For bongs/pipes: these are typically inhaled directly into the lungs. Breathe in steadily but gently. And stop drawing smoke when you’re about 3/4 through, then either lift the bowl of your bong or remove your finger from the carb hole of a pipe and draw until you clear the smoke and breathe fresh air. Then exhale steadily. Bongs will get you higher, faster since you smoke more at once/take larger hits. Same with pipes (at least for me). Take a single hit and pass. And like with joints, just wait and see between each hit so you don’t overdo it For vapes and e-nails: also inhaled directly to lungs. A bit smoother than dry herb smoking, and easier to bring to a party. Pens and rigs differ, so ask how it works if you’re not sure. No one is gonna make fun of you for not knowing. Vapes feel different than a smoke high. And dabs are VERY strong, and I can’t recommend that for your first go-round. Wait until you’re more comfortable with how weed affects you before you try concentrates For edibles: less is often more, especially with low tolerances. Most gummies and candies from dispos are either 5 or 10mg. Always ask the dosage, and don’t be afraid to ask for a half or even a quarter to start with. Edibles often take longer to hit than smoking/vaping as it has to be digested first. Start very small, and wait at least ONE HOUR before you have more. If you go for another piece, wait another 30-45 minutes. Edibles can take up to 90 minutes to hit you. And they hit harder for longer. They also give more of a body high than head feeling, so if you don’t like not feeling in control of your body if and when the couch lock hits, remember that you’re just high, nothing is wrong. And your friends, if they’re good people, aren’t going to let anything happen to you. Remember to eat a little something before you get high to combat the munchies, and drink lots of water to help with dry mouth (or eat a piece of regular hard candy). Have all of your snacks and drinks and entertainment at-hand before you start so you’re not tempted to get in the car. Stoned driving is just as dangerous as drunk driving and your French fries aren’t worth it If you get too high and start to feel nauseous, touch an ice cube to your eyelids lids and cheeks. Idk why it works, but it almost instantly helps get rid of the pukey feeling and spins. Naps are also a good way to ride out the worst of an intense high. Just get comfy somewhere quiet, and remember that you’re not actually dying, you’re just high. And to-date, no one has died from weed, and you’re unlikely to be the first Never be afraid to speak up if you have a question or are suspicious of whatever form of weed you’re using (the color-sprayed flower is one to avoid like the plague). If your friends are good eggs, they’ll understand. And remember that it’s ok if you decide that weed isn’t for you or that you don’t like how it makes you feel, and it’s ok to back out after you get there if you change your mind You can still hang out and chill, you’ll just be the sober friend who then has to make sure no one spends their rent money on door dashing crumbl cookies at 2am

If you’re taking them sublingually the idea is you’re allowing it to fully absorb through your oral mucosa which helps bypass the liver and its first pass metabolism. This allows quicker onset of effects, but by bypassing the liver you’re also not allowing the cannabinoids to be broken down into the liver into various other breakdown metabolites many of which have their own effects and benefits. Like with THC for example, its main breakdown metabolites many in the liver is 11-hydroxy-THC which is many times more potent than D9 THC itself. This is why oral edibles feel different for most people vs inhaled cannabis. Some people lack these liver enzymes and thus tinctures are the next best thing as it bypasses the liver allowing the drugs to enter bloodstream directly. Now you’ll get a different ratio of cannabinoids and metabolites when taken sublingually vs orally. Sublingual will feel more akin to inhaled cannabis so either smoked or vaped. So if you do want to try tinctures make sure to allow the tincture to fully dissolve in your mouth for 5-15 minutes, try not to let the oil get diluted by saliva, as you want it to be as concentrated as possible for best absorption, and you want to try to not swallow any of it for at least 5 minutes, preferably 15minutes. One thing you can try to help with this is to place the oil between your lower cheek and gums, this is called buccal administration, but will absorb at a similar rate as under the tongue, but it has a lower chance of being accidentally swallowed or diluted by saliva. If that still doesn’t work, I’d suggest just taking the tincture orally since you said orally taking gummies had decent relief for you before. The tincture is already cannabinoids dissolved into oil, so you’ll just be able to swallow and should give you similar effect to the gummies if they’re similar in potency. Keep in mind there’s little to no regulation surrounding hemp, so there’s tons of products that say they’re X mg’s and in reality they’re either way more or way less. All depends on the brand and stuff how accurate this labeling will be, but it takes a lot of dialing in to make accurately dosed products all the time. Also if you’re using a full spectrum product then you’re getting a lot of your relief from the combination of low dose THC plus the CBD, along with a few other minor cannabinoids, along with a long list of trace cannabinoids, and potentially terpenes and flavanoids as well. So full spectrum is always the move for the best chance of relief. All that said, not all full spectrum is created equal, since the just contain whatever cannabinoids in the plant, one companies full spectrum could have 50mg CBD/2mg THC/4mg CBG per ml and another could have 50mg CBD/4mg THC/8mg CBG, which can make a huge difference as well. Whatever strain or mix of biomass they used will have a huge impact on the final product. So try the tincture again this time bucally for 15min, if still no relief then try swallowing the tincture after eating a light meal with a decent amount of fat. If you still have no relief then the tincture you got is either weaker then the gummies, or the flower they used to make them doesn’t help your condition as much as the flower used for the gummies. Hope this helps friend, take care!!

The realm of weed these days includes flower, concentrates, edibles, and topicals. I have no experience with topicals. Flower is obviously just the dried/cured flowers from the cannabis plant. This is great to smoke in bowls, bong, and joints. It can also be vaped several ways which is also great. Bongs work by filtering some of the water soluble particles out of the smoke, as well as making sure plant matter doesnt get into your mouth/throat/lungs like can happen with pipes and poorly rolled joints. Concentrates include dabbable things like carts, as well as BHO/Rosin/etc... Dabbing is heating up some kind of thing, usually quartz ceramic or metal, then putting a cannabis concentrate on it to vaporize it. Concentrates also includes things like bubble hash and dry sift hashes, as well as other things. These arent quite as 'dabbable,' probably don't mess with these for a while. They are a little bit more challenging to smoke. You can just put some on the top of your bowls or roll them into joints though. Edibles are edibles. There are a lot of pure THC edibles, as well as edibles made from more full spectrum cannabis plant extracts. The pure THC edibles are more stimulating in general, the more full spectrum ones are more like what you would expect. Start with like 5-10mg, but if you have a tolerance you will need 2-4x more at least. There is no best way to get high. Experiment with several different methods, and you will find some you strongly prefer. It is worth it to buy bongs, rolling papers, making edibles, buying edibles, buying an oil rig for dabs, etc.... You will find some methods you strongly prefer over time. You will also notice the high from each method/type of cannabis is slightly different. You might also find that you might smoke concentrates for a few months, then swap back to bong or joints for a while, then back, etc.. Maybe you find you really like edibles and mostly use those. I'm sure I left out some popular smoking methods, but none are really 'the best.' They pretty much all have pros/cons and slightly different effects. Vaping or dabs might be better if you need to be up and stimulated, where a joint or bong might be better if your just sitting and chilling.

If you live in a cold climate or tend to get cold easily this is something to keep in mind. Feeling cold would sometimes trigger my paranoia and make me feel very self-conscious about my body. Solution? Run your hands under hot water. Take a bath. Make some hot tea. To that end, there are a lot of “normal” physical responses that your body will have to THC and I found learning about them made them less anxiety inducing when they happened. Plus, they all pass in time. 2. Tolerance helps! Once I built up a little bit of a tolerance I was able to control the level of high I wanted to be at better so I could enjoy the effects I like without going to the dark place I sometimes go to when I get too high. 3. Consumption method matters. Your high experience will not be the same flower to dab to vape to edible. If you’re testing the waters again, I’d personally encourage you not to use edibles because the arc of the high is quite long. If you’re having a bad time it will stay with you for a while. Though the high that dabs give you have a significantly shorter arc (tends to last 45-1hour for me) they can also be very intense. If you had access to a dispensary I’d recommend specifically flower with a THC % between 17%-23% or get a rosin vape. Consider what you hope to gain - invite positive experiences and embrace “negative” ones - and honestly someone suggested you grow your own and I think that’s an excellent option if you don’t have it near by. Do not get gas station Delta-8 products. This is not a good equivalent.

Clogging happens with disposables and with regular vape carts, and most places that sell carts will also sell compatible batteries for them. You can also find them at places that sell cigarettes or nicotine vapes, because the cartridges are similar. They might be cheaper at those stores. I've read many different claims as to what causes them to clog and how to prevent it, from the cartridge quality to oil quality, temperature settings on batteries, how the vape is stored, etc. As for how to fix clogs once they happen, heating up the oil might make it less viscous to allow air through again. If your disposable/vape battery has a button, usually if you press it twice it will heat the oil prior to using and that can be helpful for some people too. Personally I've only had carts start to clog up once they reached pretty much no distillate left, but a common thing I've seen from my friends is their vapes leaking and clogging more if they were left in a hot car.

No, bad idea. 4.6mg of THC in gel capsule. You think that whole capsule is 4.6mg? It's not. It's 4.6mg of THC mixed with a carrier oil. Trying to weigh out exactly 4.6mg and dispensing it into a capsule would be damn near impossible. So when you make tinctures, or in this case gel capsules, THC must be mixed with a carrier oil. These carrier oils are safe to digest through your gut, but bringing it up to its boiling point and inhaling the vapors, that's a big no-no.

The fast and dirty way to make oil is with dry ice hash. I'm going to weigh in why this is popular (at least around Europe). Nicotine in tobacco smoke literally paralyzes the cilia in the lungs, little hairlike things that filter and move germs and particulates out of the lungs. When smoking weed simultaneously, or immediately after, more THC gets through because it is unfiltered by the cilia. Literally one gets a stronger dose. Of course one gets more of the bad stuff in tobacco smoke also. Maybe this works for other cannabinoids, idk for sure. I believe this also happens with vaping. Cilia paralysis one of the reasons nicotine is so harmful, your bronchial tubes can't function normally when exposed. IF you are a very occasional smoker, it's probably not so bad and there's theories that the cannabinoids actually have anti-cancer properties that counteract the tobacco smoke. I don't think there's much science behind this, although cannabis-only smokers have unexpectedly a lot less lung cancer than tobacco smokers and I don't think it's clear to researchers exactly why that may be. I don't really see the point of smoking CBD with tobacco (or nicotine vapes for that matter). You're not going to feel it the way one feels extra THC, and it's not usually a social event: sharing CBD buds, but again idk, YMMV. Cheers!

I'd like to add to the "always go with manufacturer guidance". I'm a CBD retailer (3rd party products), it'd be super easy for me to say "use as the bottle says" but I know you can get good response as a first time user from a minimal dose (we start everyone on 5mg dosage with a full-spectrum oil). The manufacturer wants you rinsing through your bottle and back in for more. You WILL build a tolerance to CBD over time with continued use as anything. Now it's a slow process, but you'll speed that up by jumping in with a high dose. You can always work up but not down! Some people do need a higher dose dependant on time, severity of ailment or just their bodies functioning. If the dose used has done the job for you, stick with that dose twice or even three times (if needed) a day. You will build a level of CBD in the body and the effects will build and stack over the following weeks/months. If after a few days, you find that dose isn't cutting the mustard in day time, up the dose slightly. CBD isn't cut-and-dry Q&A. I have customers that swear by a particular oil whilst others don't like oils at all and prefer capsules, vapes or patches etc. Some customers use it once a day, some three times. I used to be twice a day (anxiety myself) and now I don't even use it everyday! It's very much a suck it and see thing when it comes to CBD from method of administration, dosage and frequency of doses. If you want to understand how CBD works with the body, research "The Endocannabinoid System". Add " and xxx (any ailment)" to the end of that (GAD in your case) and it will pull up ample research, articles and case studies specifically regarding that. The Endocannabinoid System is the largest system in the body that regulates almost every action within the body. There's alot to take in when it comes to CBD, if you're after guidance or any information, give me a shout by all means and I'll help where possible. It's the whole reason I've got the shop! But stick with it and you'll soon have a love and appreciation for CBD as all of us do, have a gooden 💚

I fully understand the concept of varying dosage and dosage frequency from one individual to another based upon ailment, severity and biomechanics. I will always explain that to folk as I go over initial dosage. We use the phrase "suck it and see" all the time, what works for some folk doesn't necessarily work for all. Try a few till you find the right one. If we're talking oil here, one of the most prominent. We start near enough everyone on a 5mg dose to be used morning and evening and further if required. Whether that's a broad-Spectrum or a full-spectrum content. At the same time, I will explain that some get a great initial response immediately, some not so much and in those cases to stick at it a number of days to let a level build up and kick the system into gear. From there if they don't think they've had the full response, we say to work upwards. Depending on what a customer is in for, we may suggest a higher strength and dosage initially. Typically cancers, we look to stronger oils. However I've got an old ex-speedway racer with crushed vertebrae, slipped disks, broke this, fractured that. Who had spent around £200 on CBD elsewhere, we knew these only used Isolated CBD products at this time, stuck him on a full-spec 5mg dose and it absoloutely spanked anything else. Anxiety raises your heart rate. I have had plenty of time periods in my life where weed is more or less anxiety inducing for me. Had the "am i dying" panic attack many times in my past. I've found lowering the THC level and raising the CBD level of what I'm smoking, pretty much solved my issues. And for whatever reason, even disregarding the CBD levels, citrus strains just seem to be less anxious for me personally. Also, just smoking less sounds like a duh, but that helps too if nothing else does. Also... so wait, are you saying you get actually dehydrated or just have a dry mouth when you smoke? Dehydration will 1000% make anxiety worse, weed or no weed. Drink water. If you mean you mouth gets dry... that's what weed does, I suggest... drinking some water. Can weed give you a heart attack, idk maybe technically. Technically I'm sure so could a really bad panic attack in an unlucky/predisposed person. It's astronomically more likely that the weed/panic attack is just making you feel like you're going to have a heart attack, that's actually extremely common. Stress certainly isn't great for your heart, but accepting the possibility that you could be having a heart attack but also the reality that you're probably not is actually probably much better for your heart than obsessing over whether or not this time is different from all the other times you didn't end of dying of a heart attack when you were worried you might. I'm not trying to sound like I'm judging you or picking on you or anything, I used to struggle with the "I'm gonna die" panic attacks on weed, and these are the changes I made and conclusions I came to that seemed to reduce my anxiety to the point of not really being something I stress about anymore if I want to smoke. If nothing I've said resonated with you, I guess you could just go see a cardiologist?

Weighing in here as someone who has worked in the cannabis/hemp industry for over 7 years. Delta-9 is the form of THC found in natural cannabis. In the plant it is delta-9-THCA, if it is heated or oxidized long enough it decarboxylates into Delta-9-THC. With that out of the way there is a lot more going on nowadays. Just because a product contains delta-9 THC does not mean it is naturally derived from cannabis. A ton of hemp labs around the US and other countries are converting CBD to delta-9 using a similar process to creating delta-8 THC (basically heating a polar solvent/CBD solution in the presence of a Lewis acid, usually PTSA, Glacial Acetic acid, or Hydrochloric acid). Converted delta-9 usually uses much more complex processes and, in my opinion, more dangerous catalyst ( aluminum isopropoxide or triisobutyl aluminum to name a few) and solvents like DCM or Chloroform. A good lab will water wash and neutralize these reactions and create very clean “safe” products but many do not. I generally stay clear of any “hemp legal” delta-9 THC products. Another common loophole now is THCA products gummies, vapes, flower, etc. These products are usually made from standard THC producing cannabis. The 2018 farm bill supposedly only covered delta-9 THC levels to determine if a plant is legally hemp or cannabis. Like I stated earlier though most THC in the plant is in the form of THCA so it’s pretty easy to pass the Delta-9 THC test pre harvest and obtain a “Hemp” classification. This causes some issues there is a ton of product around that is “legally hemp” that is no different than the cannabis products in dispensary’s except it is almost entirely unregulated and the majority of testing I have seen on those materials are faked or swapped around. It is also common practice for sketchy legal cannabis labs to purchase that “hemp legal Delta-9” material and swap it out with their cannabis products that fail testing. Usually they will have material fail, swap it with clean hemp and retest. They then turn around and sell that failing cannabis as the “hemp” since the hemp market is not as regulated. I’m sure this is hard to follow or horribly written but if anyone has questions DM or respond I’ll try to clear things up.

CBD is one of many other compounds that create the "weed high" feeling, with THC being another, and arguably the primary. Early on people got really obsessed with the idea that THC was what caused the high exclusively, and we've been suffering from that shit idea ever since. It's in the same category as "Indica vs Sativa", which makes no difference, the terpene and THC/CBD/CBN/etc ratios are what differentiate highs primarily (there are almost certainly too many other factors to count, like what you are, how long ago, whether you are dehydrated or tired, your brains unique neurochemistry, etc). It's a classic case of trying to find a simple explanation for a very complex system.

Let’s unpack these claims one by one and separate the scientific facts from exaggerations or misconceptions: 1. “Being born is the most traumatic event you will ever have…” • Partly True: Birth is a significant transition for a baby. Physiologically, it involves dramatic changes—such as taking the first breath, temperature regulation outside the womb, and adapting to new stimuli like light and sound. However, calling it “the most traumatic event” is subjective. For most healthy newborns, this transition is natural and not perceived as psychological “trauma” because their brain isn’t developed enough to process experiences in that way. • Cannabis Connection: There’s no evidence that cannabis specifically addresses “birth trauma” or long-term impacts of being born. This part seems more metaphorical than scientific. 2. “Mother’s breast milk is mostly endocannabinoids!” • False: Breast milk is not “mostly” endocannabinoids. Breast milk is primarily composed of fats (lipids), carbohydrates (lactose), proteins (casein and whey), water, vitamins, and minerals, along with other bioactive compounds like antibodies. • However, endocannabinoids, such as 2-arachidonoylglycerol (2-AG), are indeed present in breast milk. These compounds are part of the body’s endocannabinoid system (ECS), which helps regulate processes like appetite, mood, and immunity. 3. “2-AG in breast milk develops the baby’s endocannabinoid system…” • True: 2-AG is one of the major endocannabinoids found in breast milk. It plays a critical role in developing the baby’s endocannabinoid system, which is essential for many physiological functions, including: • Appetite stimulation (encouraging the baby to feed). • Relaxation and comfort during feeding. • Neurological development of the baby’s ECS, which is crucial for health and homeostasis. The presence of 2-AG in breast milk has been scientifically validated and is recognized as part of the natural process of neonatal development. 4. “2-AG makes the baby have the munchies and relaxes them…” • True, but Simplified: The effects of 2-AG in newborns can be loosely compared to the “munchies” associated with cannabis because both act on the same CB1 cannabinoid receptors in the brain. These receptors stimulate feeding behavior and encourage relaxation. • Additionally, 2-AG helps regulate the baby’s sucking reflex, which prevents excessive force on the nipple and promotes effective breastfeeding. 5. “The science is here; people are afraid and misled…” • Mixed Truth: The endocannabinoid system (ECS) is indeed a fundamental biological system, and its role in health and development is increasingly recognized. However, misconceptions about cannabis and the ECS are widespread. While cannabis interacts with the ECS, equating the presence of endocannabinoids in breast milk with cannabis use oversimplifies the science. • Endocannabinoids like 2-AG are naturally produced by the body and are distinct from cannabinoids found in cannabis, such as THC and CBD. Summary: • The presence of endocannabinoids (like 2-AG) in breast milk is scientifically accurate and plays a critical role in the baby’s development, especially in stimulating appetite and soothing the baby. • However, claiming breast milk is “mostly endocannabinoids” or linking the natural ECS to cannabis use is misleading. While cannabis interacts with the same system, its effects are far more complex and context-dependent. • The notion that people are “afraid” or “misled” might stem from the stigma around cannabis, but it’s important to distinguish between the natural ECS and external substances like cannabis. The science supports the role of the ECS in health but does not advocate cannabis use based on these facts.

CBD doesn’t JUST help with anxiety and pain, but those are the most commonly advertised reasons to use it. CBN is a compound that comes from aged cannabis, originally from THC strains. It's actually a different high due to a difference in the formulation used for the drinks. They use nano-emulsions to break apart the THC particles small enough to be absorbed through the oral mucosa and in your esophagus/stomach rather than your liver. Since it absorbs mostly before reaching the liver, THC doesn't undergo its metabolism to the "edible" type of high, 11-OH-THC. Thus the high you get from drinks is fast acting, short lasting, and more similar to a vaping buzz.

What you're experiencing is super common for people who take long breaks and come back. Modern weed is WAY more potent than it was a decade ago (some strains pushing 30%+ THC), and without tolerance, it can absolutely trigger anxiety and paranoid thoughts. I've seen tons of people get convinced their partners were being shady while high, only to realize the next day they were just catastrophizing normal behavior. My advice? If you want to smoke again, start with ultra-low THC strains (like 5-10%) or mix your regular weed with CBD flower at a 1:4 ratio. Take ONE small hit and wait 15 minutes. The mistake most returners make is treating their tolerance like it used to be.

I mean, I don't believe that's so. THC distillate in and of itself ought to last about an hour, maybe 30 minutes with a high tolerance. Even bad flower now still has hundreds of cannabinoids and terpenes that influence the high usually making the more powerful, full, longer high expected with flower. There isn't really much of a difference on that front. In all honesty modern grow techniques should be producing even better stronger highs. If I had to guess what is actually going on is tolerance and variety. Flower is considerably stronger, oversmoking is easy and tolerance will build far quicker. Meaning weaker, shorter highs. When all you ever smoked one was 5% you could consume a lot in a day without pushing past your limits. When its 20% thats far easier. Especially for a regular consumer. On top of that most flower on the market has a pretty similar terpene profile. I believe strain show talked about that. You are getting far less variety in the type of highs and makes it even easier to build a tolerance to it even if you are trying what should be very different flower. If cannabis companies wanted to weaken the highs and make you buy more they wouldn't be putting out 20% bud with 0% CBD. The industry would be pumping out weaker and weaker type 2 bud. So I hate to break it to you but thca is delta 9…. Thca is the precursor to delta 9 technically, when heated or as you may know “decarboxilated/ decarbed” then it becomes delta 9. That’s why you can’t just eat a nug, you would have to bake it then eat it. What you may be experiencing is just lies from the packaging, tons of products say “thca and xyz” but in reality it’s like 2% thca and mostly d8 with maybe some cbc or cbd/cbg. Even the COA can often times be faked on flower, concentrates or whatever else. If your really curious it actually goes in this order, thca to d9 then as d9 degrades it turns into cbd and cbg. CBD and cbg can also just occur naturally in high amounts like in hemp or the male cannabis plant.

Hello. I was thinking of writing this question on a thread of its own in some sub, but I don't need tons of people judging me, just one that understands to some degree what I go through. If it's okay, I'll share some of mine. I didn't have an objectively more traumatic life than others. In fact, it was quite sheltered, the issues I faced only hurt me to the degree that they did due to my sensitivity. This is how I describe myself. Not as having condition A or B, just sensitive. I lean towards depression, anxiety and bipolar. Depression as in feeling like there is no hope. Anxiety as in being overwhelmed by stress to the point of shaking. Lastly, bipolar as in feeling things are too sad or too happy, being euphoric or disphoric. It's mostly defined as a state of a lot of energy, it feels out of control at times, and my instinct is to hide. That's where the psychosis sometimes comes in. In the times it came, it did in moments of very intense anxiety. I differentiate the two because one can get very anxious but won't necessarily feel they are losing their mind, whilst I did or feared I would. I'm talking about feeling reality is not real, or that existence should end. It's very difficult to bare. By staying away from drugs of all kinds and engaging with life, I am okay now. I still deal with anxiety and depression, but everything is manageable. I can survive. But I wanted more. I want to live, not just survive. To enjoy. To seek new job opportunities, be more calm and responsible for my family. Sometimes I feel medication or CBD could help me with that. I also think about drinking wine on occasion. I never drank it. Just some champagne when I was a child and only felt more energized and happy, nothing bad, but things changed. My anxiety and fear have developed I think. Things that my child self could tolerate I find frightening now. I just don't want to panic or be overburdened with a state I do not wish to be in. Quitting weed, especially after long-term use, can be tough, but it's absolutely doable. Here are some tips that might help: 1. Stay Hydrated and Eat Light Foods: To manage nausea, try sipping on ginger tea or peppermint tea - both are known for their soothing effects. Small, bland meals like crackers or rice can also help. 2. CBD as an Alternative: Smoking CBD could help with cravings, but it’s a personal choice. If you go this route, look for high quality flower or oil with no additives. Edibles or tinctures might be gentler on your system. 3. Relaxation Techniques: Anxiety can be rough, so consider mindfulness practices like meditation or deep breathing exercises. Lavender essential oil (not vapes) can also have a calming effect. 4. Non Smoking Alternatives: Herbal blends with chamomile, mullein, or peppermint can mimic the act of smoking without harmful side effects. They’re usually mild and don’t taste like detergent! 5. Distract Yourself: Keep busy with hobbies, exercise, or something creative. The more occupied your mind is, the easier it will be to push past cravings. 6. Talk to Someone: Whether it’s a counselor, support group, or trusted friend, sharing your experience can make the process feel less isolating. 7. Prepare for Sleep Changes: If you have trouble sleeping, try natural remedies like melatonin or valerian root. A solid nighttime routine can also help regulate your sleep cycle. Remember, you're not alone, and it's okay to ask for support. You've got this! 💪
